Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 1 Smart Sensors and Devices What is a Sensor?? A sensor is a device that measures a physical quantity and converts into a signal which can be read by an observer or by an instrument. The signal produced by the sensor is equivalent to the quantity to be measured. The origin of the word sensor comes from the Latin sentire, it means to feel. Classification of sensors Basically, the sensors are classified into active and passive sensors. Active sensors Sensors that require power supply are called as active Sensors. Examples: Electrochemical sensors, and digital clinical devices (Glucometer, pulse meter etc.,) Passive sensors Sensors that do not require power supply are called as Passive Sensors. Example: Hg thermometer, piezoelectric sensor, and disposable pregnancy test kit etc., Types of Sensors: Temperature Sensors: Thermocouples: (ex of Passive) Measure temperature by generating voltage depending on the temperature difference between two points. Thermistors: (ex of Active) Changes resistance with temperature change. Pressure Sensors: Strain Gauges: Measure force by detecting changes in electrical resistance when an object is stre

two points. Thermistors: (ex of Active) Changes resistance with temperature change. Pressure Sensors: Strain Gauges: Measure force by detecting changes in electrical resistance when an object is stretched. Piezoelectric Sensors: Generate an electric charge when exposed to mechanical stress. Light Sensors: Photodiodes: Convert light into an electrical signal. LDRs (Light Dependent Resistors): Change resistance based on the amount of light hitting them. Motion Sensors: Accelerometers: Detect acceleration or movement (used in phones for detecting orientation). Gyroscopes: Measure angular velocity to track rotation. Chemical Sensors: pH Sensors: Measure the acidity or basicity of a solution.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 2 Piezoelectric Sensors: Piezoelectric sensors are special devices that turn mechanical pressure (like pushing, pulling, or vibrating) into an electrical signal. They operate based on the piezoelectric effect. They use materials (like quartz or special ceramics) that produce electricity when they are squeezed or stretched. Materials Used: Natural Piezoelectric Materials: Quartz, Rochelle salt, tourmaline. Synthetic Materials: PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate) – a ceramic material, PVDF (Polyvinylidene Fluoride), transition metal chalcogenides(S–Mo–Se) and other ceramics and polymers. Types of Piezoelectric Sensors: Pressure Sensors: Used to measure changes in pressure and convert them into electrical signals (like in car tires or machinery). Vibration Sensors: Detect mechanical vibrations and generate corresponding electric signals, often used in monitoring machinery health (used in machines to check if something is shaking too much). Ultrasonic Sensors: Generate high-frequency sound waves (ultrasound) and detect echoes, used in medical imaging and non-destructive testing (like in medical ultrasound machines). Piezoelectric sensors -

trasonic Sensors: Generate high-frequency sound waves (ultrasound) and detect echoes, used in medical imaging and non-destructive testing (like in medical ultrasound machines). Piezoelectric sensors - Principle and mechanism: Basic Principle: Piezoelectric Effect: The piezoelectric effect is the ability of certain materials to generate an electric charge in response to applied mechanical stress. Components: Piezoelectric Material: The core element (e.g., quartz, PZT). Electrodes: Conductive surfaces that collect the generated charge. Signal Processing Unit: Converts the electrical signal for measurement. Working Mechanism: 1. Crystal Structure: Asymmetry: Piezoelectric materials have a unique crystal structure that is non- centrosymmetric, meaning there is no center of symmetry in the arrangement of their atoms. This asymmetry is crucial for the piezoelectric effect. Polarization: In the absence of stress, these materials have positive and negative charge centers that are balanced, resulting in no net charge. 2. Application of Mechanical Stress: When mechanical stress (such as compression, tension, or shear) is applied to the piezoelectric material (such as quartz or certain cerami

ulting in no net charge. 2. Application of Mechanical Stress: When mechanical stress (such as compression, tension, or shear) is applied to the piezoelectric material (such as quartz or certain ceramics), it causes a deformation in the crystal lattice. This deformation shifts the positions of the positive and negative charge centers within the material. 3. Charge Displacement: Shift of Charge Centers: The applied stress causes the internal arrangement of ions to become uneven, leading to a separation of positive and negative charges.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 3 Surface Charge: As a result of this separation, an electric field is generated, and free charges accumulate on the surfaces of the material. 4. Voltage Generation and signal processing: The accumulated surface charges create a voltage across the material. This voltage can be measured as an electrical signal. The amount of generated charge (and thus the voltage) is proportional to the magnitude of the applied mechanical stress. Applications: Industrial Monitoring: To monitor the condition of machines by sensing vibrations and pressure changes. Medical Applications: For ultrasound imaging, which helps doctors see inside the body, infusion pumps to measure the pressure and flow of medicine. Consumer Electronics: To detect movement or vibration (used in accelerometers for screen rotation, Used in microphones, smartphones, gaming controllers). Aerospace and Automotive: To measure pressure in the engine or brakes. Energy Harvesting: Conversion of mechanical energy (from vibrations or movement) into electrical energy for powering small devices. Advantages: High Sensitivity: Piezoelectric sensors can detect very small changes in force,

ersion of mechanical energy (from vibrations or movement) into electrical energy for powering small devices. Advantages: High Sensitivity: Piezoelectric sensors can detect very small changes in force, pressure, or acceleration. Wide Frequency Range: They can measure dynamic changes across a broad range of frequencies, making them suitable for vibration and shock detection. Rugged and Durable: These sensors are often robust, withstanding harsh environments and extreme conditions. Compact Size: Due to their small form factor, they can be integrated into miniature devices and systems. Disadvantages: Can't Measure Constant Pressure: They only work when pressure or vibration is changing. They can’t measure pressure that stays the same (static). Temperature Sensitivity: They can be affected by high or low temperatures, which might change their accuracy.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 4 Electrochemical Sensors According to IUPAC, an electrochemical sensor is defined as “a self-contained integrated device, which is capable of providing specific quantitative or semi-quantitative analytical information” OR a device, that convert the information associated with electrochemical reactions (the reaction between an electrode and analyte) into an applicable qualitative or quantitative signal. Electrochemical sensors are based on the measurement of changes in current, potential, conductance due to the interaction of the target molecule with the electrode materials on the sensing surface. Principle of Electrochemical sensors 1. The electrochemical sensor consists of transducer component covered by recognizer component (receptor). 2. The recognizer element chemically interacts with analyte and signal is generated. 3. The electrochemical transducers transform the chemical signal into electrical signals. 4. The increase/decrease in current/potential/resistance will be directly proportional to the concentration of analyte. Types of Electrochemical Sensors: Amperometric Sensors: Amperometric sensors are sensitive analytical s

e in current/potential/resistance will be directly proportional to the concentration of analyte. Types of Electrochemical Sensors: Amperometric Sensors: Amperometric sensors are sensitive analytical systems that measure current as a result of an electroactive substance that loses (oxidation) or gains (reduction) an electron while undergoing an electrochemical reaction. These sensors measures the current between working/indicator and reference electrode which will be directly proportional to the concentration of the species that is oxidised or reduced. This works on the basis of Ohm’s Law. Example: Oxygen sensors in cars or medical devices, where oxygen interacts with the electrode to produce a current. Used in food analysis, chemical analysis, environmental analysis etc.,..

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 5 Potentiometric Sensors: Measure the voltage difference between two electrodes, often used for detecting changes in ion concentration. The potentiometric sensor measures the potential of a working or indicator electrode (Pt) by coupling with secondary reference electrode (SCE or Ag/AgCl). The potential of the working electrode will be dependent on a particular analyte and its concentration in the solution. Example: Glass electrode (pH sensors that measure the acidity or basicity of a solution) Conductometric Sensors: These sensors measure the conductivity of the medium, which is directly related to the amount of particular ions present in the medium. Example: Gas Sensors (that detect gases like ammonia or CO2 based on changes in electrical conductivity), to detect acidic components in engine oil, etc.,. Advantages of electrochemical sensors The electrochemical sensors have several advantages like, • simple, • low cost, • portable, • rapid, • no sample pretreatment involving tedious steps are required, • highly sensitive and selective, • possess lower detection limits (LODs) and • very quick with respect to analysis time in real

rapid, • no sample pretreatment involving tedious steps are required, • highly sensitive and selective, • possess lower detection limits (LODs) and • very quick with respect to analysis time in real samples Applications of electrochemical sensors The range of applications where electrochemical sensors are in use are: • gas sensors, such as those used in homes to detect CO, • heavy metal sensors for water quality analysis (arsenic, cadmium, nickel, mercury, chromium, zinc, and lead), • pH (H+ ion monitoring), • biosensors used for monitoring glucose, cholesterol, urea, enzymes, proteins, amino acids etc., • hydrocarbon, alcohol, and ketone sensors for measuring motor oil degradation, • to detect the presence of toxic gases such as H2S, Cl2, and SO2, and variation of oxygen in the air, • Pharmaceutical compositions etc.,. Disadvantages: Limited Lifespan: Electrochemical sensors can degrade over time as the chemicals involved in the reaction are consumed. Temperature and Humidity Sensitivity: Performance may be affected by environmental conditions. Calibration Required: They often need regular calibration to maintain accuracy.
vity: Performance may be affected by environmental conditions. Calibration Required: They often need regular calibration to maintain accuracy.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 6 Nanomaterials for Sensing Applications Definition: The materials having at least one dimension in the nanoscale (approximately 1-100 nm) are called nanomaterials. They have unique properties (optical, mechanical, electrical, thermal), which are very different from the properties the material has at the macroscale. Due to their high surface area, enhanced electrical, thermal, and mechanical properties, make them highly effective for sensing applications. Why Use Nanomaterials for Sensors? High Surface Area: Nanomaterials have a large surface area to volume ratio, which allows for more interaction with the target substance (gas, chemical, or biological molecule). Enhanced Sensitivity: Nanomaterials can detect even minute changes in their environment, making them highly sensitive. Fast Response Time: Due to their small size and high reactivity, nanomaterial-based sensors often respond quickly to any changes. Gas Sensors for NOx and SOx Detection Gas Sensors: Gas sensors detect and measure the concentration of gases in the environment by reacting with the gas and converting it into a measurable signal (usually electrical). They are

x Detection Gas Sensors: Gas sensors detect and measure the concentration of gases in the environment by reacting with the gas and converting it into a measurable signal (usually electrical). They are widely used in environmental monitoring, industrial safety, and automotive exhaust systems. NOx and SOx gases: NOx (Nitrogen Oxides): Harmful gases like NO, NO2 are pollutants from vehicles and industries. SOx (Sulfur Oxides): SO2 and SO3 are gases released from burning fossil fuels and industrial processes, contributing to acid rain. Different Nanomaterial Sensors for detecting NOx and SOx gases: Metal Oxide Nanoparticles sensors: When NOx or SOx gases interact with metal oxide nanomaterials, their electrical resistance changes. This change is measured to determine the gas concentration. Nanomaterials like ZnO, TiO2, SnO2, and WO3 are commonly used in gas sensors because of their sensitivity to gases like NOx and SOx. 2D Nanomaterials: Graphene and CNT Sensors: These materials have high electrical conductivity, high surface area, and sensitivity to chemical adsorption making it suitable for gas detection. When nitrogen oxides or sulfur oxides gases interact on the surface of graphene

electrical conductivity, high surface area, and sensitivity to chemical adsorption making it suitable for gas detection. When nitrogen oxides or sulfur oxides gases interact on the surface of graphene or CNTs, leads to charge transfer which in-turn changes the material’s electrical properties, these interactions enables the gas detection. 2D nanomaterials like Graphene, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), and fullerenes are highly conductive and hence they can detect gases even at very low concentrations. To enhance the sensitivity and selectivity of gas sensors, further addition of nanoparticles like platinum, silver or gold to above 2D nanomaterials, increases the reaction with NOx and SOx gases, thereby improving sensing performance.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 7 Applications Air Quality Monitoring: Detecting harmful NOx and SOx levels in the environment. Industrial Safety: Monitoring gases in chemical plants or factories to prevent accidents. Automotive Sensors: Used in exhaust systems to monitor and reduce harmful emissions. Biosensors for Glucose sensing Biosensors are devices that use biological molecules (like enzymes or antibodies) to detect specific substances, converting their presence into an electrical, optical, or thermal signal. Commonly used for medical diagnostics, food quality control, and environmental monitoring. Glucose Sensors: Glucose sensors measure the glucose concentration in blood, commonly used by diabetic patients to monitor their sugar levels. Working mechanism: Glucose sensors typically use an enzyme called glucose oxidase that reacts with glucose. This reaction produces hydrogen peroxide, which is detected by the sensor and converted into an electrical signal that corresponds to glucose concentration. The hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) generated in the reaction is detected electrochemically. When H₂O₂ is oxidized at the sensor electrode, it produces electrons (e⁻)

at corresponds to glucose concentration. The hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) generated in the reaction is detected electrochemically. When H₂O₂ is oxidized at the sensor electrode, it produces electrons (e⁻), which generate a current proportional to the glucose concentration. Nanomaterials used in Glucose Sensors: Carbon-Based Nanomaterials: Carbon Nanotubes (CNTs): CNTs provide high surface area and excellent conductivity, improving the sensitivity of glucose sensors. Graphene: graphene improves the sensitivity of sensors by allowing rapid electron transfer during the glucose detection process, due to its high electrical conductivity and large surface area.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 8 Metallic Nanoparticles: Nanoparticles such as gold (AuNPs) are used to immobilize the enzyme on the sensor surface, increasing the reaction efficiency with glucose. They enhance electron transfer processes in electrochemical reactions. Nanostructured Metal Oxides: Materials like ZnO and TiO2 help improve the electrochemical properties of glucose sensors. These Nanomaterials improve the electrochemical activity of glucose sensors by facilitating faster electron transfer, which leads to a more rapid response to glucose levels. The high surface area of nanomaterials allows for more active sites for enzyme immobilization, which increases the sensitivity of the sensor to glucose. Applications: Diabetes Monitoring: Glucose sensors are an essential tool for diabetics, allowing them to monitor their blood sugar levels. Wearable Health Devices: Nanomaterial-based glucose sensors can be incorporated into wearable devices for continuous glucose monitoring. Non-Invasive Sensors: Ongoing research into non-invasive glucose sensors aims to develop patches or contact lenses that can measure glucose from sweat or tears. Advantages of Nanomateri

se monitoring. Non-Invasive Sensors: Ongoing research into non-invasive glucose sensors aims to develop patches or contact lenses that can measure glucose from sweat or tears. Advantages of Nanomaterial-Based Glucose Sensors: High Sensitivity: Low detection limit, Nanomaterials help to detect very small concentrations of glucose. Fast Response Time: Due to the large surface area and excellent conductivity of nanomaterials, the sensors can provide real-time readings. Miniaturization: Nanomaterials allow glucose sensors to be smaller and integrated into portable or wearable devices.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 9 RFID and IONT materials RFID (Radio Frequency Identification) RFID systems are widely used for tracking, identification, and data exchange in various industries such as logistics, retail, healthcare, and security. They use electromagnetic fields to automatically identify and track tags attached to objects. RFID tags consist of a microchip (for storing information) and an antenna (for transmitting the data). IoNT (Internet of Nano Things) The Internet of Nano Things (IoNT) refers to a network of interconnected nano-sized devices that communicate data and interact with larger systems, much like the Internet of Things (IoT) but on a nanoscale. This technology is highly advanced and applicable in areas like healthcare, environmental monitoring, energy management, and logistics. Synthesis, Properties, and Applications of Carbon Nanotubes (CNTs) in Logistic Information and intelligent packaging systems Carbon Nanotubes (CNTs) Carbon Nanotubes (CNTs) are cylindrical nanostructure composed of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice, forming tube-like structures. They can be classified as: Single-Walled Carbon Nanotubes (SWCNTs): C

tubes (CNTs) are cylindrical nanostructure composed of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice, forming tube-like structures. They can be classified as: Single-Walled Carbon Nanotubes (SWCNTs): Consist of a single graphene sheet rolled into a tube. Multi-Walled Carbon Nanotubes (MWCNTs): Comprise multiple layers of graphene sheets rolled concentrically. CNTs possess extraordinary electrical, thermal, and mechanical properties, making them versatile for various applications, including logistics and packaging systems. Synthesis of Carbon Nanotubes: Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) method: Catalyst: Metal catalysts (e.g., iron, cobalt, nickel) act as nucleation sites for CNT growth. Carbon Source: Hydrocarbons like acetylene (C₂H₂), ethylene (C₂H₄), and methane (CH₄) serve as carbon sources.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 10 Temperature: Typically conducted at 600°C to 1000°C to facilitate decomposition and growth. Process: A carbon-containing gas (like methane or acetylene) is passed over a substrate coated with a metal catalyst (usually nickel, cobalt, or iron) at high temperatures. The catalyst helps decompose the carbon source, and the product CNTs is formed on the substrate. Advantages: High yield, scalable, and commonly used for industrial production. Disadvantages: May produce CNTs with impurities that require purification. Properties of Carbon Nanotubes Electrical Properties: CNTs can be either metallic or semiconducting depending on their structure. High conductivity: Metallic CNTs exhibit excellent electrical conductivity Mechanical Properties: Strength: CNTs are incredibly strong, with tensile strength much higher than steel. This is due to presence of covalent sp2 bonds formed between the individual carbon atoms. Elasticity: They are also highly elastic, making them resistant to deformation under stress. Thermal Properties: High thermal conductivity: CNTs are excellent conductors of heat, with thermal conductivity values higher than di

highly elastic, making them resistant to deformation under stress. Thermal Properties: High thermal conductivity: CNTs are excellent conductors of heat, with thermal conductivity values higher than diamond. Temperature stability: They can withstand high temperatures without degradation. Chemical Properties: Chemical inertness: CNTs are chemically stable and resistant to most acids and bases. Functionalisation: Surface functionalization can be done to tailor CNTs for specific chemical interactions, improving their application in sensors, logistics, and more. Lightweight: CNTs have a low density, making them ideal for lightweight, high-strength applications.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 11 Applications of Carbon Nanotubes in Logistic Information As Sensors for Real-Time Tracking and Monitoring: CNTs can be used as smart sensors for tracking and monitoring logistics systems (supply chains, transportation, inventory, etc.). Wireless Sensors: With CNTs’ high electrical conductivity and sensitivity, they are ideal for creating wireless sensors that monitor conditions like temperature, humidity, and pressure during the shipping of goods. Vibration and Stress Sensors: CNT-based sensors can detect mechanical stress or vibrations in transport containers, preventing damage to goods. RFID Tags: CNTs are used in Radio Frequency Identification (RFID) tags due to their small size and conductive properties. CNTs improve the efficiency of RFID systems by providing better signal transmission, thus allowing for more accurate tracking of shipments. Nanocomposite Packaging: CNTs are incorporated into packaging materials to create lightweight and strong protective materials for delicate or high-value items. Improved Barrier Properties: CNT-infused packaging materials can offer better protection against moisture, oxygen, and other e

tweight and strong protective materials for delicate or high-value items. Improved Barrier Properties: CNT-infused packaging materials can offer better protection against moisture, oxygen, and other environmental factors, extending the shelf life of perishable goods during transport. Smart Labels and Barcodes: CNTs are used in printed electronics to create flexible, smart labels and barcodes that can be embedded with sensors. These labels can provide real-time data on the condition of goods during transit, allowing companies to ensure proper handling and storage. Applications of Carbon Nanotubes in Intelligent Packaging Systems Intelligent Packaging refers to packaging that has the capability to monitor, sense, and communicate the condition of the product inside the package. It is designed to provide real- time information on product quality, safety, and environmental conditions during storage and transport. Carbon Nanotubes (CNTs), due to their exceptional mechanical, electrical, and thermal properties, have been widely integrated into intelligent packaging systems, providing innovative solutions for industries like food, pharmaceuticals, and logistics. Key Applications of CNTs in

thermal properties, have been widely integrated into intelligent packaging systems, providing innovative solutions for industries like food, pharmaceuticals, and logistics. Key Applications of CNTs in Intelligent Packaging Systems: 1. Sensors for Monitoring Environmental Conditions Temperature and Humidity Sensors: CNTs are used to create conductive films or thin layers that detect changes in temperature or humidity inside the packaging. These sensors provide real-time data, helping to ensure that the product is stored under optimal conditions. Example: In food packaging, CNT sensors can monitor temperature changes during transport, preventing spoilage or degradation.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 12 Gas Sensors: CNTs are highly sensitive to gases like oxygen (O2), carbon dioxide (CO2), and ammonia (NH3). They can be used to detect gas leakage or changes in the internal atmosphere of the packaging, indicating spoilage or contamination. Example: In vacuum-sealed packaging, CNT-based sensors can detect oxygen levels, ensuring that the seal is intact and the product remains fresh. 2. Smart Labels for Real-Time Monitoring Radio Frequency Identification (RFID) Tags: CNTs can be used in the development of flexible RFID tags that provide real-time information on product location, condition, and history. Example: In pharmaceutical packaging, RFID tags with CNTs can track the distribution of medicine, ensuring that it remains within the correct temperature range. 3. Improved Mechanical and Barrier Properties Strengthening Packaging Materials: CNTs can be incorporated into packaging materials to enhance mechanical strength and flexibility. This allows for lighter, more durable packaging that can better protect the product from external physical damage during transport. Example: In industrial packaging, CNT-reinforced materials can b

ty. This allows for lighter, more durable packaging that can better protect the product from external physical damage during transport. Example: In industrial packaging, CNT-reinforced materials can be used to protect delicate electronic components from mechanical stress and impacts. Enhanced Barrier Properties: CNTs provide enhanced gas and moisture barrier properties, preventing the entry of unwanted gases or moisture that could degrade the product. Example: In food and beverage packaging, CNT-based materials can prevent oxygen from penetrating the packaging, extending the shelf life of the product. 4. Anti-Counterfeiting and Authentication CNTs can be used in conductive inks to create secure, non-replicable markings or tags on packaging. Example: In luxury goods packaging, CNT-based markings ensure the product’s authenticity, providing consumers with peace of mind regarding the legitimacy of their purchase. Conductive CNT coatings can be applied to packaging surfaces to create interactive labels or markings that authenticate the product when scanned. These coatings can change their electrical properties when exposed to certain conditions, creating a security feature that is diff

active labels or markings that authenticate the product when scanned. These coatings can change their electrical properties when exposed to certain conditions, creating a security feature that is difficult to duplicate. 5. Self-Healing and Smart Packaging Materials Self-Healing Coatings: CNTs can be incorporated into materials that exhibit self-healing properties when exposed to damage. This ensures that any minor scratches, punctures, or other damages to the packaging are automatically repaired, maintaining the integrity of the packaging. Example: In high-value electronics or pharmaceutical packaging, CNT-based self-healing materials can ensure that any micro-damages to the packaging during transport are automatically repaired, protecting the contents.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 13 Synthesis, Properties, and Applications of Polyaniline (PANI) in Logistic Information and Intelligent Packaging Systems Polyaniline (PANI) is a conducting polymer that has gained significant attention due to its unique properties, ease of synthesis, environmental stability, and tunable electrical conductivity. These properties make it suitable for a variety of applications, including sensors, electronic devices, and intelligent packaging systems. 1. Synthesis of Polyaniline (PANI) The synthesis of PANI can be achieved through several methods, including chemical oxidation, electrochemical polymerization, and emulsion polymerization. The chemical oxidative polymerization of aniline is the most common method. Chemical Oxidative Polymerization method: Monomer: Aniline (C₆H₅NH₂) Oxidizing Agent: Commonly ammonium persulfate (APS) in an acidic medium (HCl or H₂SO₄). Process: Aniline is dissolved in an acidic aqueous medium. APS is added as the oxidizing agent, which initiates the polymerization of aniline. The reaction produces emeraldine salt, the most conductive form of PANI. The polymerization process is typically conducted at lo

as the oxidizing agent, which initiates the polymerization of aniline. The reaction produces emeraldine salt, the most conductive form of PANI. The polymerization process is typically conducted at low temperatures (0-5°C). The polymer is filtered, washed, and doped to achieve the desired properties. Properties of Polyaniline (PANI) PANI exhibits several properties that make it highly versatile for applications in logistics and intelligent packaging: a. Electrical Conductivity: PANI has a tunable conductivity that can range from insulating to metallic, depending on its doping level and oxidation state. Its emeraldine salt form is the most conductive form

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 14 b. Environmental Stability: PANI is stable in air and exhibits good chemical resistance, making it suitable for use in a variety of environmental conditions. c. Thermal Properties: PANI shows thermal stability up to moderate temperatures, which is sufficient for most logistic and packaging applications. d. Optical Properties: The polymer has notable optical properties, including changes in color with changes in its oxidation state. This makes PANI useful for sensors that respond to environmental changes. e. Flexible and Lightweight: As a polymer, PANI is naturally flexible and lightweight, which is critical for applications in flexible electronics, smart labels, and packaging systems. f. Chemical Sensitivity: PANI is sensitive to changes in pH, gas exposure, and chemical doping, making it an excellent material for use in chemical sensors. Applications of Polyaniline (PANI) in Logistic Information Smart Packaging: PANI is used in packaging that can monitor conditions like temperature and humidity. This helps ensure products are kept in safe conditions during transport. Built-in Sensors: PANI sensors in the packaging can provide

ackaging that can monitor conditions like temperature and humidity. This helps ensure products are kept in safe conditions during transport. Built-in Sensors: PANI sensors in the packaging can provide real-time information about the product's environment. Anti-Counterfeiting: Security Tags: PANI can be added to labels or tags that verify the authenticity of products, making it harder to counterfeit them. Color Change Indicators: PANI can change color when tampered with, signaling potential counterfeiting. RFID Tags: Tracking Goods: PANI can be used in RFID tags, which help track products in shipping and storage. This improves efficiency in logistics. Flexible Electronics: PANI’s properties allow for the creation of flexible RFID sensors that can fit on various surfaces. Monitoring Conditions: Temperature and Humidity Sensors: PANI-based sensors can check the environment for sensitive goods, like food and medicines, during transport. Smart Labels: Conductive Inks: PANI can be used in printed electronics, which allow for the creation of low- cost circuits that help transmit information. Labels made with PANI can store and share details about the product’s journey, like where it is an

inted electronics, which allow for the creation of low- cost circuits that help transmit information. Labels made with PANI can store and share details about the product’s journey, like where it is and how it should be handled.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 15 Corrosion Protection Anti-Corrosion Coatings: PANI can be applied as a coating material for metal components in logistics (such as shipping containers or electronic packaging), offering protection against corrosion. Example: Metal parts in packaging systems (e.g., for electronics) can be coated with PANI to prevent corrosion during long-term storage or transport in humid conditions. Data Monitoring and Communication in Logistics PANI-based materials can be integrated into smart packaging to provide real-time information on product condition during transit. This helps streamline logistics by ensuring goods are transported under optimal conditions. Example: PANI sensors embedded in packaging monitor temperature, gas levels, and humidity. The data collected is communicated wirelessly to logistics personnel via RFID tags, ensuring that perishable goods are stored and transported safely. Application of PANI in Intelligent Packaging Systems Condition Monitoring: Sensors: PANI can be used in sensors that check the temperature and humidity inside packages. This helps keep products like food and medicine safe. Real-Time Information: Th

tems Condition Monitoring: Sensors: PANI can be used in sensors that check the temperature and humidity inside packages. This helps keep products like food and medicine safe. Real-Time Information: These sensors give immediate updates, ensuring products are stored correctly. Indicator Labels: Color Change Indicators: PANI can change color if conditions inside the package are not right. This alerts people if a product might be spoiled or damaged. Expiration Indicators: Labels made with PANI can change color as the product gets close to its expiration date, helping consumers know when to use it. Anti-Counterfeiting Features: Security Tags: PANI can be included in tags that verify if a product is real or fake, making it harder to copy products. Embedded Electronics: PANI can be used in small circuits within the packaging to track where the product has been and confirm its authenticity. Sustainable Packaging: Eco-Friendly: PANI can be made from renewable resources, helping create more environmentally friendly packaging. Biodegradable Options: When mixed with other materials, PANI can help create packaging that breaks down naturally. Communication with Consumers: Smart Labels: PANI can

ronmentally friendly packaging. Biodegradable Options: When mixed with other materials, PANI can help create packaging that breaks down naturally. Communication with Consumers: Smart Labels: PANI can be part of smart labels that provide important information about the product, such as how to store it and where it comes from.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 1 Redox flow batteries Redox flow batteries (RFBs) represent one class of electrochemical energy storage devices. They are a type of rechargeable battery that store energy in liquid electrolytes. They are unique compared to conventional batteries because their energy storage capacity can be easily scaled up by increasing the size of the storage tanks. Classification of redox flow batteries Redox flow batteries differ primarily in the composition of the electrolytes and the solvents. Redox couple Cell voltage Hydrogen bromine 1.1 V Iron-chrome 1.2 V Vanadium/polyhalide 1.3 V Vanadium/Vanadium 1.4 V Bromine polysulphide 1.5 V Zinc-Bromine 1.8 V Hydrogen-Bromine Redox Flow Battery Consists of Hydrogen and bromine. How it works: Hydrogen is oxidized, and bromine is reduced to store energy. Iron-Chromium Redox Flow Battery Consists of Iron on one side and chromium on the other. How it works: Iron and chromium changes forms to store and release energy. Vanadium Polyhalide Redox Flow Battery Consists of Vanadium in one half-cell and halides (like chlorine or bromine) in the other. How it works: Vanadium changes oxidation states in the v

lease energy. Vanadium Polyhalide Redox Flow Battery Consists of Vanadium in one half-cell and halides (like chlorine or bromine) in the other. How it works: Vanadium changes oxidation states in the vanadium half-cell, and halides (such as bromine) undergo redox reactions in the other half-cell. All-Vanadium Redox Flow Battery (VRFB) Consists of Vanadium in both sides of the battery. How it works: Vanadium changes its form (oxidation state) during charging and discharging. Polysulfide-Bromine Redox Flow Battery Consists of Polysulfide and bromine. How it works: Polysulfide and bromine react to store energy. Zinc-Bromine Redox Flow Battery Consists of Zinc and bromine. How it works: Zinc gets plated (deposited) on the electrode during charging, and bromine is produced.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 2 Construction and Working of Vanadium Redox Flow Battery The vanadium redox flow battery is one of the most promising secondary batteries as a large- capacity energy storage device for storing renewable energy. A vanadium redox flow battery (VRFB) is a specific type of redox flow battery that uses vanadium ions in different oxidation states to store and release energy. Its key advantage lies in using the same element (vanadium) for both the positive and negative half-cells, which prevents cross-contamination of electrolytes, a common issue in other flow batteries. Principle: The electrolyte in the positive half-cell contains vanadium in the +5 and +4 oxidation states (VO₂⁺/VO²⁺), while the electrolyte in the negative half-cell contains vanadium in the +3 and +2 oxidation states (V³⁺/V²⁺). These reactions involve the transfer of electrons, converting electrical energy to chemical energy and vice versa. Construction: 1. Electrolytes: o The electrolyte is prepared by dissolving vanadium pentoxide (V2O5) in H2SO4. Both the electrolytes are stored in separate tank. The positive half-cell electrolyte contains VO²⁺ (V4+) and VO₂⁺ (V5+)

o The electrolyte is prepared by dissolving vanadium pentoxide (V2O5) in H2SO4. Both the electrolytes are stored in separate tank. The positive half-cell electrolyte contains VO²⁺ (V4+) and VO₂⁺ (V5+) and the other negative half- cell electrolyte contains V²⁺ and V³⁺ 2. Electrochemical Cell Stack: o The stack consists of multiple cells with electrodes where the redox reactions occur. These cells are separated by an ion-selective membrane, which allows ions (typically protons) to flow between the two half-cells while keeping the electrolytes separated. 3. Ion-Selective Membrane: o This membrane (e.g., Nafion) allows protons (H⁺ ions) to pass through while preventing the vanadium ions from passing, maintaining separation between the positive and negative electrolytes. 4. Pumps and Reservoirs: o Pumps circulate the vanadium electrolyte solutions from external tanks through the cell stack, facilitating continuous operation.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 3 Working Mechanism: 1. Charging Process: The reaction progresses as current flows from positive electrode to negative electrode. At the positive electrode (cathode): V+4 à V5+ + e- In this reaction, VO²⁺ (vanadium in +4 oxidation state) is oxidised to VO₂⁺ (vanadium in +5 oxidation state) At the negative electrode (anode): V3+ + e- à V2+ Here, V³⁺ (vanadium in +3 oxidation state) gains the electron, thereby it is reduced to V²⁺ (vanadium in +2 oxidation state). 2. Discharging Process: During discharging, the reaction progresses as current flows from negative electrode to the positive electrode. At the negative electrode (anode): V2+ à V3+. + e- Here, V²⁺ (vanadium in +2 oxidation state) is oxidised to V³⁺ (vanadium in +3 oxidation state) At the positive electrode (cathode): V+5 + e- à V4+ In this reaction, VO₂⁺ (vanadium in +5 oxidation state) gain an electron and is reduced to VO²⁺ (vanadium in +4 oxidation state) Since the charging and discharging reactions are taking place only due to the exchange of electrons between the ions in the electrolyte, so it can be said that the redox flow batteries are batteries that will not dete

g and discharging reactions are taking place only due to the exchange of electrons between the ions in the electrolyte, so it can be said that the redox flow batteries are batteries that will not deteriorate. Advantages: 1. High safety - The electrolyte used is non-flammable. (even positive and negative liquids are mixed will not ignite) 2. Eco friendly – No electrolyte degradation. Electrolyte can be recycled and reused 3. Long life -No need to replace battery 4. Low life cycle cost – As electrolyte can be recycled and reused, the life cycle cost is low with long term capacity 5. Easy operation 6. Flexible design – number of cell stack can be increased and electrolyte tank size can be increased. Applications: 1. It is used in power stations for storing electricity 2. It can be used for storing renewable energy 3. It helps in reducing the power bills in industries 4. It is eco-friendly as it do not release toxic gases.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 4 Introduction to Next-Generation Sodium-Ion Batteries (SIBs) As the global demand for energy storage rises—especially for applications like electric vehicles (EVs) and renewable energy storage—there is an increasing need for sustainable and cost- effective alternatives to lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). Sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) have emerged as a promising contender due to the abundance of sodium, lower costs, and potential for environmental benefits. The working principle of Sodium-Ion Batteries (SIBs) is similar to that of lithium-ion batteries. Construction and Working Principle of SIBs: Anode: Usually made of hard carbon or other carbon-based compounds. Cathode: Transition metal oxides (such as NaMnO₂) or polyanionic compounds (like Na₃V₂(PO₄)₃). Electrolyte: Sodium salts like NaPF₆ or NaClO₄ dissolved in organic solvents serve as the electrolyte, facilitating the movement of sodium ions between the electrodes. Working Principle: Charging: Sodium ions (Na⁺) de-intercalate from the cathode and travel through the electrolyte to intercalate into the anode. Discharging: The sodium ions flow back from the anode to the cathode, ge

: Charging: Sodium ions (Na⁺) de-intercalate from the cathode and travel through the electrolyte to intercalate into the anode. Discharging: The sodium ions flow back from the anode to the cathode, generating electricity. This movement of sodium ions between the electrodes drives the charging and discharging processes. Advantages of Sodium-Ion Batteries over Lithium-Ion Batteries: 1. Abundance of Sodium: Sodium is the sixth most abundant element on Earth, making up roughly 2.6% of the Earth's crust. In contrast, lithium is much rarer, constituting about 0.002%. 2. Cost-Effectiveness: The raw materials needed for sodium-ion batteries, especially sodium, are cheaper compared to those used in lithium-ion batteries. Sodium is readily available and can be sourced from inexpensive materials like seawater, which leads to potentially lower production costs compared to lithium-ion batteries. 3. Environmental Benefits: The mining and extraction processes for sodium are less environmentally damaging than lithium, cobalt, and nickel, which are often used in LIBs. 4. Thermal Stability and Safety: Sodium-ion batteries are inherently safer than LIBs due to their superior thermal stability. This r

ing than lithium, cobalt, and nickel, which are often used in LIBs. 4. Thermal Stability and Safety: Sodium-ion batteries are inherently safer than LIBs due to their superior thermal stability. This reduces the risk of thermal runaway—a dangerous condition where batteries overheat and potentially catch fire or explode.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 5 Challenges in Sodium-Ion Batteries: 1. Lower Energy Density: Sodium-ion batteries generally have lower energy density than lithium-ion batteries. Since, Sodium atoms are larger and heavier than lithium atoms, it reduces the amount of energy that can be stored in a given volume or weight of the battery. 2. Limited Commercial Availability: Although there has been significant research in SIBs, the technology is not as mature as lithium-ion, meaning commercial-scale production is still developing. 3. Anode and Cathode Material Limitations: Traditional graphite anodes used in LIBs are not compatible with sodium-ion batteries, leading to the need for alternative materials like hard carbon or other carbon-based compounds. Applications of Sodium-Ion Batteries (SIBs) 1. Grid Energy Storage: Used to store excess energy from renewable sources (solar/wind) and balance electricity supply during peak demand. 2. Electric Vehicles (EVs): Suitable for short-range EVs like e-bikes and scooters, focusing on affordability and environmental benefits. 3. Consumer Electronics: Potential for use in smartphones, laptops, and portable devices, offering

for short-range EVs like e-bikes and scooters, focusing on affordability and environmental benefits. 3. Consumer Electronics: Potential for use in smartphones, laptops, and portable devices, offering faster charging and improved safety. 4. Backup Power Systems: Ideal for uninterruptible power supplies (UPS) in critical infrastructures like hospitals and data centers ensuring continuous operation. 5. Industrial Applications: Useful in forklifts, telecom networks, and other industries needing reliable backup power or sustainable energy solutions. Overview of Battery Technology for E-Mobility E-mobility refers to the electrification of transportation, primarily through the use of electric vehicles (EVs) powered by rechargeable batteries. The performance of these vehicles is largely dependent on battery technology. Lithium-Ion Batteries (LIBs): Most common in electric vehicles (EVs). Advantages: High energy storage, fast charging capabilities, and long lifespan. Disadvantages: High cost, safety concerns (thermal runaway), and reliance on rare materials like lithium, cobalt, and nickel. Solid-State Batteries: Future technology replacing liquid electrolytes with solid ones. Advantages: H

afety concerns (thermal runaway), and reliance on rare materials like lithium, cobalt, and nickel. Solid-State Batteries: Future technology replacing liquid electrolytes with solid ones. Advantages: Higher energy density, improved safety, longer lifespan, and reduced risk of overheating. Disadvantages: Expensive and still under development for commercial viability.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 6 Nickel-Metal Hydride (NiMH): Used in hybrid vehicles (HEVs), not full electric vehicles. Advantages: Affordable and reliable. Disadvantages: Lower energy density compared to LIBs, making them unsuitable for long- range EVs. Sodium-Ion Batteries (SIBs): Emerging technology as a cheaper alternative to LIBs. Advantages: Abundant raw materials (sodium), safer, and more environmentally friendly. Disadvantages: Lower energy density than LIBs, still in the development stage. IoT-Based Battery Monitoring System for Electric Vehicles (EVs) An IoT-based Battery Monitoring System is crucial for ensuring the safety, reliability, and efficiency of electric vehicles by continuously tracking the battery's health and performance in real time. This helps in, 1. Real-Time Data Collection: IoT sensors continuously monitor key battery parameters like temperature, voltage, current, and state of charge (SOC). 2. Remote Monitoring: Vehicle owners and fleet managers can check battery status anytime using a phone app or website. 3. Predictive Maintenance: IoT systems use data analytics to predict potential problems (like battery failure) before they ha

et managers can check battery status anytime using a phone app or website. 3. Predictive Maintenance: IoT systems use data analytics to predict potential problems (like battery failure) before they happen, so that it can be fixed early. 4. Battery Health Management: IoT helps monitor battery degradation over time, ensuring optimal performance and extending battery life. 5. Energy Optimization: The system can suggest to use the battery more efficiently by providing suggestions on when to charge or discharge. 6. Alerts and Notifications: The system can send alerts in case of abnormalities, such as overheating or overcharging, reducing risks of battery damage or failure.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 7 Battery Management System Li-ion battery hazards The key hazards associated with Li-ion batteries are: Overheating & Thermal Runaway: Battery can overheat and catch fire or explode due to Overcharging, high temperature, or short circuits. Fire Hazard: Damaged batteries can trigger a fire due to physical damage, high heat, or defects in manufacturing. Toxic Chemical Leakage: Damaged or improperly handled Li-ion batteries can leak toxic electrolytes. These chemicals are harmful to humans and the environment, causing skin burns, respiratory issues, and environmental pollution. This may happen due to improper charging cycles or Degradation due to overuse. Explosion Risk: Battery can explode if it overheats too much due to overcharging leading to gas buildup, and exposure to extreme temperatures or physical impact. Short Circuiting: Can cause overheating or battery failure. This may be due to the faulty internal design or external factors like damaged insulation Or Contact between battery terminals due to improper handling or storage. Electrochemical Recovery of Lithium from Spent Batteries Recovering lithium from used lithium-ion b

rs like damaged insulation Or Contact between battery terminals due to improper handling or storage. Electrochemical Recovery of Lithium from Spent Batteries Recovering lithium from used lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) is important for recycling and sustainability. Steps involved in the process are: 1. Disassembly of Spent Batteries: Used batteries are carefully taken apart to separate different parts, especially the cathode (which contains lithium). 2. Leaching Lithium: The cathode material is treated with acids (like sulfuric or hydrochloric acid combined with oxidants like hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂).) to dissolve lithium into a solution. 3. Electrochemical Recovery: Lithium ions (Li⁺) are recovered from the leached solution using electrochemical techniques like electrodeposition or electrodialysis. Electrodeposition: Lithium is deposited onto an electrode by applying an electric current through the leachate solution containing lithium ions. This forms metallic lithium or lithium compounds on the cathode. Electrodialysis: An electric field is applied across a membrane, driving lithium ions through the membrane to be collected on the other side. This is a separation process used for se

s on the cathode. Electrodialysis: An electric field is applied across a membrane, driving lithium ions through the membrane to be collected on the other side. This is a separation process used for selective ion recovery. 4. Post-Processing of Lithium: After electrochemical recovery, the lithium product need to be refined or converted into commercially valuable lithium compounds like lithium

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 8 carbonate (Li₂CO₃) or lithium hydroxide (LiOH). This is to make lithium suitable for making new batteries. Best storage and handling practices of Li ion batteries, and their Safe disposal: Best Storage Practices: Keep Cool: Store in a cool, dry place, ideally between 15°C and 25°C. Avoid hot or freezing places, which can damage the battery. Partially Charged: Store batteries at about 40% to 60% charge. This helps extend their life. Dry Area: Store in a place without moisture to avoid corrosion or damage. Keep Away from Fire: Store far from flammable objects. Best Handling Practices: Always use the charger made for the device to avoid overheating. Handle Carefully as it may cause them to overheat or catch fire. Unplug once the battery is fully charged and don’t leave it charging overnight. Check for bulging, leaking, or cracks. If so to stop using the battery immediately. Safe Disposal of Li-ion Batteries: Safe Disposal of Li-ion Batteries is important because: Electrolytes used in LIBs can react with moisture or air to form hydrofluoric acid (HF), a highly corrosive and toxic substance. Improper disposal can lead to these dange

tteries is important because: Electrolytes used in LIBs can react with moisture or air to form hydrofluoric acid (HF), a highly corrosive and toxic substance. Improper disposal can lead to these dangerous chemicals seeping into soil and water, causing environmental damage and health risks. Preventing Fires and Explosions: Li-ion batteries are highly reactive and can easily ignite if punctured or exposed to high temperatures. The organic solvents used in the electrolyte are highly flammable. Heavy Metal Contamination: Metals like cobalt, nickel, and manganese can be harmful to ecosystems. Cobalt is especially toxic and can cause both environmental pollution and human health issues if not disposed of correctly. Some safe disposal tips are: 1. Neutralizing Electrolytes: The electrolytes need to be neutralized to prevent them from reacting with the environment. This is done through chemical treatments that decompose the harmful compounds into less hazardous forms. 2. Recovery of Valuable Metals: Electrochemical, Hydrometallurgical or pyrometallurgical processes are used to recover metals like cobalt, nickel, lithium, and aluminum from batteries.
etals: Electrochemical, Hydrometallurgical or pyrometallurgical processes are used to recover metals like cobalt, nickel, lithium, and aluminum from batteries.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 9 Using acids (like sulfuric acid) to dissolve metals for recovery. Then the metals are precipitated from solutions or recovered using electrical currents. 3. Preventing the Formation of Toxic Compounds: When Li-ion batteries are incinerated improperly, they can produce toxic fumes, including dioxins and furans. Recycling centers use controlled environments to prevent the formation of these toxic compounds.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 1 MODULE – 5 Vapor Corrosion Inhibitors (VCIs) in Protecting Computer Circuit Boards Vapor Corrosion Inhibitors (VCIs) are an effective solution for protecting computer circuit boards from corrosion caused by environmental factors such as humidity, air, salt, and pollutants. Vapor Corrosion Inhibitors : VCIs are chemicals that release corrosion-inhibiting vapors, which form a protective molecular layer on metal surfaces. This layer prevents the metal from undergoing electrochemical reactions that lead to corrosion. Types of VCIs Used for Circuit Boards Anodic Inhibitors: o These inhibitors work by forming a protective oxide layer on the anode of the metal, preventing further oxidation and corrosion. Examples: Calcium nitrite, sodium nitrite, sodium benzoate, and sodium chromate Cathodic Inhibitors: These work by reducing the rate of the cathodic reaction (e.g., oxygen reduction or hydrogen evolution). They often work by either: o Precipitating protective compounds (e.g., calcium, magnesium) on the cathode surface to block the reaction. o Reduce the cathodic reaction by blocking oxygen or precipitating protective compounds. Exampl

cipitating protective compounds (e.g., calcium, magnesium) on the cathode surface to block the reaction. o Reduce the cathodic reaction by blocking oxygen or precipitating protective compounds. Examples: Carbonates, Phosphonates, Polyphosphates Mixed Inhibitors: o These inhibitors provide protection by acting on both the anodic and cathodic reactions simultaneously. They form a uniform protective film over the entire metal surface. Examples: Amine-based inhibitors, silicates, benzotriazole (for copper). How VCI work? 1. VCIs are embedded in various carriers such as sealed pack/ enclosed pack. 2. Vapor Corrosion Inhibitors (VCIs) work by releasing corrosion-inhibiting molecules into the enclosed environment

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 2 3. Once the VCI molecules come into contact with a metal surface, they adsorb onto it, forming a molecular protective layer. 4. This layer prevents corrosion by blocking the interactions of the metal with moisture, oxygen, and other corrosive agents. Example of VCI Application in Circuit Boards For sensitive electronics like circuit boards: 1. The board is placed in a VCI-infused bag or box. 2. The VCI molecules diffuse and adsorb onto the metal contacts and traces, forming a protective layer against corrosion. 3. The protection remains active as long as the environment is enclosed. 4. Upon opening, the VCIs evaporate, leaving the board ready for immediate use. Benefits of VCI Technology: 1. Non-Invasive: Protects even hard-to-reach areas without direct application. 2. Clean Protection: Leaves no residue, unlike oils or coatings. 3. Ease of Use: Integrated into packaging materials or small capsules. 4. Cost-Effective: Reduces the need for additional protective coatings or maintenance. Thermal Management in CPUs Using Liquid Cooling Systems Liquid cooling is a thermal management strategy that uses a liquid coolant to transfer he

eed for additional protective coatings or maintenance. Thermal Management in CPUs Using Liquid Cooling Systems Liquid cooling is a thermal management strategy that uses a liquid coolant to transfer heat away from a CPU and other computer components. Liquid cooling systems use a circulating liquid (usually water or a water-based coolant) to transfer heat away from the CPU to a radiator or a heat exchanger, where it is dissipated into the surrounding environment. During normal operation, the transistors inside a CPU convert electrical energy into thermal energy (heat). This heat increases the temperature of the CPU. If an efficient path for that heat doesn’t exist, then the CPU will exceed its safe operating temperature.” Hence, CPUs needs to be properly cooled to achieve maximum performance. Components and Working: • Coolant: The medium that absorbs and transports heat. Often water-based with additives that absorbs heat from the CPU's. • Water Block: It is a component mounted on the CPU to absorb heat through direct contact. • Pump: Circulates the coolant through the system. • Radiator: Transfers heat from the coolant to the air, often using fans to increase airflow.
to absorb heat through direct contact. • Pump: Circulates the coolant through the system. • Radiator: Transfers heat from the coolant to the air, often using fans to increase airflow.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 3 • Tubing: Connects the components and facilitates coolant flow. The working of a liquid cooling system for thermal management in CPUs can be explained in four key steps: 1. Heat Absorption: o A water block is mounted on the CPU. This block absorbs heat generated by the CPU through a layer of thermal paste that improves thermal conductivity. 2. Heat Transfer: o A pump circulates the liquid coolant through the tubing, transporting the absorbed heat away from the CPU to the radiator. 3. Heat Dissipation: o In the radiator, the heated coolant releases its heat to the surrounding air. Fans attached to the radiator help to enhance this heat dissipation process by improving airflow. 4. Coolant Recirculation: o The cooled liquid is then recirculated back to the water block on the CPU, and the cycle repeats. Applications • High-Performance Computing (HPC): o For tasks like video editing, 3D rendering, and scientific simulations. • Gaming: o Overclocked gaming rigs often use liquid cooling to sustain performance. • Data Centers: o Large-scale systems utilize liquid cooling to efficiently manage heat in densely packed servers.
Overclocked gaming rigs often use liquid cooling to sustain performance. • Data Centers: o Large-scale systems utilize liquid cooling to efficiently manage heat in densely packed servers.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 1 E-waste management E-waste refers to electronic or electrical goods which can no longer be used. They may contain hazardous or toxic material which cannot be disposed with other solid waste. Sources of e-waste: It includes mobile phones, batteries, telephones, switch, socket, cable wires, computer hardware’s, Large household appliances like refrigerators/freezers, washing machines, dishwashers, televisions, Small household appliances which include toasters, coffee makers, irons, hairdryers etc, and other electronic products which are not of use. Hazards and Toxicity of E-Waste E-waste contains hazardous substances that can harm human health and the environment if not handled properly. Hazardous Components: Heavy Metals: • Lead: Found in cathode-ray tubes and solder. Causes neurological and developmental issues. • Mercury: Present in fluorescent lamps and LCD screens. Toxic to the nervous system. • Cadmium: Found in batteries and semiconductors. Causes kidney damage and is carcinogenic. Plastics: • Non-biodegradable plastics release harmful gases like dioxins when burned. Other Toxins: • Polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs) and phth

miconductors. Causes kidney damage and is carcinogenic. Plastics: • Non-biodegradable plastics release harmful gases like dioxins when burned. Other Toxins: • Polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs) and phthalates in old electronics can disrupt hormones and cause cancer. Health Impacts: • E-waste can be toxic, contains various types of toxic metals (lead, mercury, nickel, cadmium, lithium etc.), which can get accumulated in the environment, in the soil, air, water. This can cause a long-term damage to the environment. Humans rely on air to breathe, so when that air fills with toxins, it can affect a person’s respiratory system. • The improper disposal of lead acid batteries and alkaline batteries also causes contamination of toxic chemicals with the soil and water. Lead may get into the blood, kidneys and even the nervous system and can show adverse effects. • When a pregnant woman is exposed to such harmful substances, it can affect the development and health of her unborn child. Adverse health effects to the child include stillbirth, premature birth and low birth weight.
lth effects to the child include stillbirth, premature birth and low birth weight.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 2 Environmental Impacts: • Soil and water contamination from leached heavy metals and chemicals. • Air pollution from burning plastics and improper disposal. • Bioaccumulation of toxins in the food chain. Segregation and Recycling of E-Waste E-waste (electronic waste) includes discarded electronic devices like computers, mobile phones, and appliances. Effective recycling of e-waste is essential to recover valuable materials, reduce environmental harm, and promote sustainability. The process involves segregation followed by recycling using techniques such as hydrometallurgy, pyrometallurgy, and direct recycling. 1. Segregation of E-Waste Segregation is the first and most critical step in e-waste recycling. It separates different materials to improve recycling efficiency and ensure hazardous substances are managed safely. Steps in Segregation: 1. Manual Sorting: Handpicking materials like plastic, glass, and metals. 2. Magnetic Separation: Using magnets to extract ferrous metals. 3. Eddy Current Separation: Removing non-ferrous metals (e.g., aluminium, copper). [Eddy Current Separation (ECS) is a non-ferrous metal separation techni

ion: Using magnets to extract ferrous metals. 3. Eddy Current Separation: Removing non-ferrous metals (e.g., aluminium, copper). [Eddy Current Separation (ECS) is a non-ferrous metal separation technique used primarily in recycling to recover materials like aluminium, copper, and other conductive metals from a mixture of waste. It relies on the principles of electromagnetic induction to separate metals from non-metallic materials]. 4. Density-Based Separation: Using air or water to separate materials by weight. 5. Chemical Analysis: Identifying precious metals like gold, silver, and palladium in printed circuit boards (PCBs). 2. Recycling Techniques (a) Hydrometallurgy Hydrometallurgy is a chemical-based process that uses aqueous solutions to extract metals from e-waste. Detailed Steps: Leaching: Metals such as gold, silver, copper, and palladium are dissolved from e-waste components like PCBs using chemical agents such as: • Cyanide: Commonly used for gold and silver recovery.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 3 • Aqua Regia: A mixture of nitric acid and hydrochloric acid, effective for dissolving precious metals. • Thiourea or Thiosulfate: Environmentally friendly alternatives to cyanide. This process selectively targets metals while leaving other materials relatively intact. Solution Purification: Impurities are removed from the leachate using techniques such as: • Precipitation: Adding chemicals to form insoluble compounds that can be filtered out. • Solvent Extraction: Separating desired metals into an organic phase for further processing. • Ion Exchange: Using resin to capture specific metal ions from the solution. Metal Recovery: Extracted metals are recovered in pure form through: • Electrowinning: Applying an electric current to deposit metals onto electrodes. • Chemical Reduction: Adding reducing agents to precipitate metals from the solution. Advantages: 1. High recovery efficiency for precious and base metals. 2. Operates at relatively low temperatures, saving energy. 3. Versatile for processing various types of e-waste. Challenges: 1. Use of toxic chemicals like cyanide and aqua regia requires strict safety measures and pro

atively low temperatures, saving energy. 3. Versatile for processing various types of e-waste. Challenges: 1. Use of toxic chemicals like cyanide and aqua regia requires strict safety measures and proper disposal protocols. 2. The process can be time-consuming and requires precise control of chemical conditions. 3. Management of chemical waste is essential to prevent environmental harm. Applications: 1. Recovering gold, silver, platinum, and palladium from printed circuit boards and connectors. 2. Extracting base metals like copper and nickel from e-waste components. 3. Recycling lithium-ion batteries to recover cobalt, lithium, and manganese. (b) Pyrometallurgy Pyrometallurgy involves using high temperatures to extract metals from e-waste. It is particularly suitable for bulk processing and materials that are difficult to separate using other methods. Detailed Steps are: 1. Pre-Treatment: • E-waste is shredded or crushed to reduce its size and increase surface area for processing.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 4 • Hazardous components such as batteries or capacitors may be removed to prevent explosions or toxic releases during heating. 2. Smelting: • The shredded e-waste is subjected to high temperatures in a furnace (800–1,200°C). 3. Refining: • Removing impurities from the molten metal. 4. Gas Treatment: • Emissions generated during smelting (e.g., dioxins, furans, and heavy metal vapors) are captured and further treat these emissions to minimize environmental harm. Advantages: • Efficient for processing large volumes of e-waste. • Recovers metals that are difficult to extract using other methods, such as copper, nickel, and zinc. • Capable of processing mixed and contaminated materials. Challenges: • High energy consumption. • Releases harmful gases if not properly managed. Applications: • Recycling bulk metals like copper, aluminum, and lead from e-waste. • Recovering precious metals such as gold and silver. (c) Direct Recycling Direct recycling involves reusing materials or components with minimal processing. This method is energy-efficient and prioritizes preserving the integrity of the original materials or components. Detailed

recycling involves reusing materials or components with minimal processing. This method is energy-efficient and prioritizes preserving the integrity of the original materials or components. Detailed Steps: 1. Disassembly and Inspection: Devices are manually or mechanically disassembled to recover usable parts. Components are inspected to determine functionality and potential for reuse. 2. Cleaning and Refurbishment: Recovered components are cleaned to remove dust, debris, or residues. Damaged components are repaired 3. Reintegration: Refurbished components are reintegrated into new or existing devices. Usable raw materials (e.g., plastic casings, wiring) are processed for manufacturing new products. Advantages: • Energy-efficient and cost-effective. • Preserves the original material properties.

Department of Chemistry, BMSIT&M, Dr Jyothi C. Abbar 5 Challenges: • Limited to uncontaminated or minimally damaged components. • Not suitable for components that have degraded significantly or are highly integrated. Applications: • Refurbishing old devices for resale. • Recycling lithium-ion batteries by reusing electrode materials. Extraction of gold from E-waste: E-waste can often comprise of several hazardous components which can include heavy metals like mercury and lead. Despite this reality, gold, as well as other precious metals, also make up a significant amount of e-waste, and its extraction could have advantageous properties for the gold industry. The extraction of gold is done from e-waste using Hydrometallurgy method. Prior treatment: Prior to the Hydrometallurgy method, physical treatments such as sorting, dismantling and size reduction are performed to produce granular form of the waste material. Hydrometallurgy method: There are certain metals such as copper (Cu), silver (Ag) and gold (Au) are extracted by dissolving the concentrated e-waste in some suitable reagent and then rescuing the metal from the solution by treatment with some more electropositive metal. The

r (Ag) and gold (Au) are extracted by dissolving the concentrated e-waste in some suitable reagent and then rescuing the metal from the solution by treatment with some more electropositive metal. The use of cyanide in gold leaching has been a useful, but dangerous technique of metal extraction. This extraction process involves the chemical reaction between the pulverized e-waste and sodium cyanide, which produces a soluble gold cyanide solution that allows for easier extraction of the precious metal. 4 Au + 8NaCN + 2H2O --------> 4Na[Au(CN)2] + 4NaOH Then the solution containing Gold cyanide complex is treated with zinc powder. As zinc is more electropositive than Au, it results in the precipitation of pure Au. 2Na[Au(CN)2] + Zn --------> Na2[Zn(CN)4] + 2Au
