Introduction to Electronics & Communication (BESCK104C) Module 4: Embedded Systems – Definition, Embedded systems vs general computing systems, Classification of Embedded Systems, Major application areas of Embedded Systems, Elements of an Embedded System, Core of the Embedded System, Microprocessor vs Microcontroller, RISC vs CISC. Sensors and Interfacing – Instrumentation and control systems, Transducers, Sensors, Actuators, LED, 7-Segment LED Display. 4.1 Embedded Systems Definition An embedded system is an electronic/electro-mechanical system designed to perform a specific function and is a combination of both hardware and firmware (software). Every embedded system is unique and the hardware as well as the firmware is highly specialized to the application domain. Embedded systems are becoming an inevitable part of any product or equipment in all fields including household appliances, telecommunications, medical equipment, industrial control, consumer products, etc. Embedded system is a combination of 3 things 1. Hardware 2. Software 3. Mechanical component & it is supposed to do only one specific task only Examples: Example1: Washing Machine A washing machine from an embedded s

is a combination of 3 things 1. Hardware 2. Software 3. Mechanical component & it is supposed to do only one specific task only Examples: Example1: Washing Machine A washing machine from an embedded systems point of view has: Hardware: Buttons, displays & buzzer, electronic circuitry. Software: It has a chip on the circuit that holds the software which drives controls & monitors various operations possible. Mechanical components: the internals of a washing machine which actually wash the clothes control the input and output of water. Example-2: Air Conditioner An Air Conditioner from an embedded systems point of view has: Hardware: Remote, display & buzzer, infrared Sensors, electronic circuitry. Software: It has a chip on the circuit that holds the software which drives control & monitors the various operations possible. The software monitors the external temperature through the sensos and then releases the coolant or suppresses it. Mechanical components: The internals of an air conditioner the motor, the outlet, etc. Dr Asha K, Dept. of ECE, BMSIT&M 1
tioner the motor, the outlet, etc. Dr Asha K, Dept. of ECE, BMSIT&M 1

Introduction to Electronics & Communication (BESCK104C) 4.2. Embedded System vs. genera computing system List the differences between General Purpose computing system and Embedded system: Parameter General purpose computing system Embedded System Contents A system which is a combination of a generic hardware and general- purpose operating system for executing a variety of applications A system which is a combination of special- purpose hardware and embedded operating system & for executing specific set of applications. OS It contains a general-purpose operating system (GPOS) It may or may not contain an operating system for functioning Alterations Applications are alterable (programmable) by the user. (It is possible for end user to re-install the OS and also add or remove user applications) The firmware of the Embedded system is pre- programmed and it is non- alterable by the end user. (Few exceptions may be present for system supporting OS kernel image flashing through special hardware settings.) Key Factor Performance is the key deciding factor in the selection of the system. Alw ays, Faster is better. Application specific requirements (like performance, power requirements, memo

re settings.) Key Factor Performance is the key deciding factor in the selection of the system. Alw ays, Faster is better. Application specific requirements (like performance, power requirements, memory usage etc.) are the key deciding factor. Power consumption More Less Response Time Response requirements are not Critical For certain category of embedded system like mission critical system the response time requirement is highly critical Execution Need not be deterministic in execution behaviour Deterministic for certain types of Embedded system like “Hard real time” systems. Dr Asha K, Dept. of ECE, BMSIT&M 2

Introduction to Electronics & Communication (BESCK104C) 4.3. Classification of Embedded Systems: The classification of embedded system is based on following criteria's: 1. Based on generation 2. Based on complexity & performance 3. Based on deterministic behavior 4. Based On triggering Let us now discuss each classification in detail 4.3.1. Classification based on generation: • First generation (1G): These embedded systems are a. Built around 8bit microprocessor (like 8085, Z80) & 4-bit microcontroller. b. Simple in hardware circuit & firmware developed in assembly code. c. Examples: Digital telephone keypads, stepper motor control unit. • Second generation (2G): These embedded systems are a. Built around 16-bit microprocessor & 8-bit microcontroller. b. They are more complex & powerful than first generation microprocessor & microcontroller. c. 2G system has embedded operating system for their operation. d. Examples: Data acquisition system, Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition System (SCADA) systems • Third generation (3G): a. With the advancement in processor technology, Embedded system developers started making 32-bit microprocessor and 16-bit microcontroller for their desig

tem (SCADA) systems • Third generation (3G): a. With the advancement in processor technology, Embedded system developers started making 32-bit microprocessor and 16-bit microcontroller for their design. b. Concepts like Digital Signal Processors (DSPs), Domain specific/Application Specific Integrated Circuits (ASICs) are evolved. c. Instruction set become more powerful and complex and also pipeline included. d. Different vendor produced processor and controller (ex. Intel Pentium, Motorola 68K have gained attention. e. Examples: Robotics, Media, Industrial process control and networking etc. • Fourth generation (4G): Dr Asha K, Dept. of ECE, BMSIT&M 3

Introduction to Electronics & Communication (BESCK104C) a. Built around 64-bit microprocessor & 32-bit microcontroller. b. The concept of System on Chips (SoC), recon figurab le pro cesso r an d Multicore Processors w ere evolved. c. Processor are bringing high performance, tight integration and miniaturization into to Embedded System d. Highly complex & very powerful systems. Examples: Smart Phones, mobile internet devices (MIDs). 4.3.2. Classification based on complexity & performance: • Small-scale: a. It is suitable for simple applications. b. Performance not time-critical. c. It’s may oy may not contain OS. d. Built around low performance & low cost 8- or 16-bit µp/µc. e. Example: an electronic toy • Medium-scale: a. Slightly complex in hardware & firmware requirement. b. Built around medium performance & low cost 16- or 32-bit µp/µc. c. Usually contain operating system. d. Examples: Industrial machines. • Large-scale: a. Highly complex hardware & firmware. b. Built around 32- or 64-bit RISC µp/µc or PLDs or Multicore Processors c. Examples: Mission critical applications. 4.3.3. Classification based on deterministic behavior: It is applicable for Real Time systems. The applica

64-bit RISC µp/µc or PLDs or Multicore Processors c. Examples: Mission critical applications. 4.3.3. Classification based on deterministic behavior: It is applicable for Real Time systems. The application/task execution behavior for an embedded system can be either deterministic or non-deterministic. These are classified in to two types: Soft Real Time Systems: Missing a deadline may not be critical & can be tolerated to a certain degree Hard Real Time Systems: Missing a program/task execution time deadline can have catastrophic consequences (financial, human loss of life, etc. Dr Asha K, Dept. of ECE, BMSIT&M 4

Introduction to Electronics & Communication (BESCK104C) 4.3.4. Classification based on triggering: These are classified into two types Event triggered: Activities within the system (e.g., task run-times) are dynamic and depend upon occurrence of different events. Time triggered: Activities within the system follow a statically computed schedule (i.e., they are allocated time slots during which they can take place) and thus by nature are predictable. 4.4 Major Application Areas of Embedded Systems: The application areas and the products in the embedded domain are countless. A few of the important domains and products are listed below:  Consumer electronics: Camcorders, cameras, etc.  Household appliances: Television, DVD players, washing machine, fridge, microwave oven, etc.  Home automation and security systems: Air conditioners, sprinklers, intruder detection alarms, closed circuit television cameras, fire alarms, etc.  Automotive industry: Anti-lock braking systems (ABS), engine control, ignition systems, automatic navigation systems, etc.  Telecom: Cellular telephones, telephone switches, handset multimedia applications, etc.  Computer peripherals: Printers, scanners

ine control, ignition systems, automatic navigation systems, etc.  Telecom: Cellular telephones, telephone switches, handset multimedia applications, etc.  Computer peripherals: Printers, scanners, fax machines, etc.  Computer networking systems: Network routers, switches, hubs, firewalls, etc.  Healthcare: Different kinds of scanners, EEG, ECG machines etc. 9. Introduction to Electronics & Communication (BESCK104C) 4.5. Elements of an embedded system: A typical embedded system contains a single chip controller which acts as the master brain of the system. Diagrammatically an embedded system can be represented as shown in figure 4.1: Embedded systems consist of a system core which can be a single chip controller. This system core will act as a brain of the system. The system core can be a microprocessor, microcontroller, FPGA, ASIC, DSP or SoC. The input signals are sensed through sensors are provided to the embedded systems through input ports by the end users to the system core. The system processes the signals and provide the control signals to ports (actuators). Keyboards, push button, switches, etc. are examples of common user interface output devices for a typical embedded system. Some embedded systems do not require any manual intervention for their operation. They automatically sense the input parameters from real world through sensors which are connected at input port. The sensor information is passed to the processor after signal conditioning and digitization. The core of the system performs some predefined operations on input d

s which are connected at input port. The sensor information is passed to the processor after signal conditioning and digitization. The core of the system performs some predefined operations on input data with the help of embedded firmware in the system and sends some actuating signals to the actuator connect connected to the output port of the system. The memory of the system is responsible for holding the code (control algorithm and other important configuration details). There are two types of memories are used in any embedded system. Fixed memory (ROM) is used for storing code or program. The user cannot change the firmware in this type of memory. The most common types of memories used in embedded systems for control algorithm storage are OTP (One Time Programmable), PROM, UVEPROM, EEPROM and FLASH. Dr Asha K, Dept. of ECE, BMSIT&M 6

General Purpose and Domain Specific Processor: Almost 80% of the embedded systems are processor/ controller based. The processor may be microprocessor or a microcontroller or digital signal processor, depending on the domain and application 4.6.1.1. Microprocessor: A silicon chip representing a Central Processing Unit (CPU), which is capable of performing arithmetic as well as logical operations according to a pre- defined set of Instructions, which is specific to the manufacturer In general, the CPU contains the Arithmetic and Logic Unit (ALU), Control Unit and Working registers Microprocessor is a dependent unit and it requires the combination of other Dr Asha K, Dept. of ECE, BMSIT&M 7

Developers of microprocessors: • Intel – Intel 4004 – November 1971(4-bit) • Intel – Intel 4040. • Intel – Intel 8008 – April 1972. • Intel – Intel 8080 – April 1974(8-bit). • Intel – Intel 8085 – 1976. 4.6.1.2. Microcontroller: A highly integrated silicon chip containing a CPU, scratch pad RAM, Special and General-purpose Register Arrays, On Chip ROM/FLASH memory for program storage, Timer and Interrupt control units and dedicated I/O ports. Microcontrollers can be considered as a super set of Microprocessors. Since a microcontroller contains all the necessary functional blocks for independent working, they found greater place in the embedded domain in place of microprocessors. Microcontrollers are cheap, cost effective and are readily available in the market. Texas Instruments TMS 1000 is considered as the world’s first microcontroller. 4.7. Differences between Microprocessor and Microcontroller: Microprocessor Microcontroller Microprocessor are widely used in computer systems. Microcontroller is widely used in embedded systems. A silicon

Differences between Microprocessor and Microcontroller: Microprocessor Microcontroller Microprocessor are widely used in computer systems. Microcontroller is widely used in embedded systems. A silicon chip representing a Central Processing Unit (CPU) embedded into it, which is capable of performing arithmetic and logical operations. It is a highly integrated chip that contains a CPU, scratch pad RAM, special and general-purpose register arrays, on chip ROM/ FLASH memory for the program stage, timer and interrupt control units and dedicated I/O ports. It is a dependent unit. It requires the combination of other chips like timers, program and data memory chips, interrupt controller for its functioning. It is a self-contained unit and does not require external interrupt controller, timer UART etc. for functioning. It consumes more power. It consumes less power than a microprocessor. Transducers: Transducers are devices that convert energy in the form of sound, light, heat, etc., into an equivalent electrical signal, or vice versa. Ex: A loudspeaker is a transducer that converts low frequency electric current into audible sounds. A microphone, on the other hand, is a transducer that performs the reverse function i.e., that of converting sound pressure variations into voltage or current. Loudspeakers and microphones can thus be considered as complementary transducers. Transducers may be used both as inputs to electronic circuits and outputs from them. Introduction to Electronics & Communication (BESCK104C) loudspeaker is an output transducer designed for use in conjunction with an audio system. A microphone is an input transducer designed for use with a recording or sound reinforcing system. 4.11. Sensors A sensor is a special kind of transducer that converts energy from one form to another for any measurement or control purpose. Ex. A Temperature sensor. The signal produced by a sensor is an electrical analogy of a physical quantity, such as distance, velocity, acceleration, temperature, pressure, light level, etc. The choice of sensor is governed by a number of factors including accuracy, resolution, cost and physical size. Sensors can be categorized as either active or passive. An active sensor generates a current or voltage output. A passive transducer requires a source of current or voltage and it modifies this in some way (e.g. by virtue of a change in the sensor’s resistance). Sensors can also be classed as either digital or analog. The output of a digital sensor can exist in only two discrete states, either ‘on’ or ‘off’, ‘low’ or ‘high’, ‘logic 1’ or ‘logic 0’, etc. 4.12. Actuators: Actuator is used for output. It is a

analog. The output of a digital sensor can exist in only two discrete states, either ‘on’ or ‘off’, ‘low’ or ‘high’, ‘logic 1’ or ‘logic 0’, etc. 4.12. Actuators: Actuator is used for output. It is a transducer that may be either mechanical or electrical which converts signals to corresponding physical actions (motion). Actuators acts as an output device. Introduction to Electronics & Communication (BESCK104C) 4.10. LED: LED is an output device for visual indication in any embedded system. LED is a p-n junction diode and contains a CATHODE and ANODE for functioning the anode is connected to positive terminal of power supply and cathode is connected to negative terminal of power supply. The maximum current flowing through the LED is limited by connecting a resistor in series between the power supply and LED as shown in the figure. There are two ways to interface an LED to a microprocessor/microcontroller: First approach, the anode of LED is directly connected to the port pin and cathode to ground and Port pin drives the LED: In this approach the port pin ‘sources’ the current to the LED when it is at logic high. In the second approach, the cathode of LED is connected to the port pin of processor/controller and anode to supply voltage (Vcc) through a current limiting resistor. LED is turned on when the port pin is at logic Low; Here the port pin ‘sinks’ current. If the LED is directly connected to the port pin depending on the maximum current at a port pin can source the brightness of LED may not be to the required level. in the secon

ort pin ‘sinks’ current. If the LED is directly connected to the port pin depending on the maximum current at a port pin can source the brightness of LED may not be to the required level. in the second approach the current is directly sources by the power supply and the port pin access the sink for the current here we will get the required brightness for the LED. LED Figure (b) LED Interfacing 4.10. 7-Segement LED Display: A seven-segment display (SSD), or seven-segment indicator, is a form of electronic display device for displaying decimal numerals that is an alternative to the more complex dot matrix displays. Dr Asha K, Dept. of ECE, BMSIT&M 13

Introduction to Electronics & Communication (BESCK104C) Seven-segment displays are widely used in digital clocks, electronic meters, basic calculators, and other electronic devices that display numerical information The seven elements of the display can be lit in different combinations to represent the Arabic numerals. Often the seven segments. Figure show the 7 segment LED display. In most applications, the seven segments are of nearly uniform shape and size (usually elongated hexagons, though trapezoids and rectangles can also be used), though in the case of adding machines, the vertical segments are longer and more oddly shaped at the ends in an effort to further enhance readability. The segments of a 7-segment display are referred to by the letters A to G, where the optional decimal point (an "eighth segment", referred to as DP) is used for the display of non-integer numbers (characters). For example, for displaying the number 4, the segments F, G, B and C are lit. For displaying 3 the segments A, B, C, D, G and DP are lit. For displaying the character ‘d’ the segments B, C, D, E and G are lit. All these eight LED segments need to be connected to one port of the processor/contr

the segments A, B, C, D, G and DP are lit. For displaying the character ‘d’ the segments B, C, D, E and G are lit. All these eight LED segments need to be connected to one port of the processor/controller for displaying alpha numeric digits. Dr Asha K, Dept. of ECE, BMSIT&M 14

Introduction to Electronics & Communication (BESCK104C) The seven segment LED displays are available in two different configurations, namely: ➢ Common Anode ➢ common cathode In common anode configuration that anodes of 8 segments are connected commonly where as in the common cathode configuration the eight LED segments share the common line. Figure illustrate the common anode and common cathode configurations. Based on the configuration of 7 segment LED unit, the LED segment’s anode or cathode is connected to the port of the processor or controller in the order ‘A’ segment to the least significant port pin and DP segment to the most significant port pin. The current flow through each of the LED segments should be limited to the maximum values supported by the LED display units. The typical value for a current fall within the range of 20 mA. The current through each segment can be limited by connecting a current limiting resistor to the anode or cathode of each segment. The value for each current limiting register can be calculated using the current value from the electrical parameter listing of the LED display. For common cathode configurations the anode of each LED segment is conn

ch current limiting register can be calculated using the current value from the electrical parameter listing of the LED display. For common cathode configurations the anode of each LED segment is connected to the port pins of the port to which the displace interfaced. The anode of the common anode LED display is connected to the 5-volt supply voltage through a current limiting resistor and the cathode of each LED segment is connected to the respective port pin lines. For an LED segment to lit in the common anode LED configuration, the port pin to which the cathode of the LED segment is connected should be set at logic zero. Seven segment LED display is a popular choice for low-cost applications like, public telephone call monitoring devices, point of sale terminal etc. Common Cathode configuration. BMS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY AND MANAGEMENT Module 3 Union and State Executive, Legislature and Judiciary, Parliamentary System, Federal System, Centre state relations, Union Executive- President, Prime Minister, Union Cabinet, Parliament – Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha, Parliamentary Committes, Important Parliamentary Terminologies, Supreme Court of India, Judicial review and Judicial Activism , State Executives- Governor, Chief Minister, State Cabinet, State Legislature, High Court and Subordinate Courts, Special Provisions (Articles 370, 371, 371J) for some states. Parliamentary System: The democratic system of government can be divided into the parliamentary and the presidential system based on the relationship between the executive and the legislature. In a parliamentary system, executive is a part of legislature, which implements the law and plays an active role in framing it as well. The parliamentary government is also called as the Cabinet government due to concentration of executive powers in the cabinet 20-12-2023 Tejaswini.B,J, Assistant Professor 1
ve powers in the cabinet 20-12-2023 Tejaswini.B,J, Assistant Professor 1

BMS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY AND MANAGEMENT 20-12-2023 2 Federal System: distribution of legislative powers between the Centre and the state • Union list: Parliament has exclusive power to legislate, 100 items like defense, armed forces, arms and ammunition, atomic energy, foreign affairs, war and peace, citizenship, extradition, railways, shipping and navigation, airways, posts and telegraphs • State list: The State legislature has exclusive power to make laws on these subjects. 61 items like maintaining law and order, police forces, healthcare, transport, land policies, electricity in the state, village administration, etc. • Concurrent list: Subjects on which both Centre and state can legislate, 52 items like marriage and divorce, education, contracts, bankruptcy and insolvency, trustees and trusts, civil procedure, contempt of court etc. Centre state relations: The Centre-state relations are divided into three parts • Legislative Relations • Administrative Relations • Financial Relations Tejaswini.B,J, Assistant Professor
tions Tejaswini.B,J, Assistant Professor

BMS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY AND MANAGEMENT 20-12-2023 3 Tejaswini.B,J, Assistant Professor • Executive- The one which executes/brings the law into force • Legislature- The organ which makes(passes or/amends) the laws • Judiciary- The authority which interprets and analyses the laws and also has power to strike any law which is ultra vires the constitution. Administration is through three organs

BMS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY AND MANAGEMENT 20-12-2023 5 Tejaswini.B,J, Assistant Professor Executives Nominal Executives President Vice President Real Executives Prime Minister and the Council of Ministers President • Qualifications • Elections • Oath, terms and conditions of service • Powers/Functions • Impeachment Qualifications ➢He/She should be a citizen of India ➢Should be more than 35 years of age ➢Should not hold any office of profit ➢Should be qualified to be a member of Lok Sabha

BMS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY AND MANAGEMENT 20-12-2023 Tejaswini.B,J, Assistant Professor 6 Elections President is indirectly elected by a special method called Proportional Representation by means of single transferrable voting system. He is elected by an electoral college which consists of elected members of the Parliament and elected members of all state legislature.

BMS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY AND MANAGEMENT 20-12-2023 7 Tejaswini.B,J, Assistant Professor Oath, terms and conditions, privileges of the President ➢President is administered oath by Chief Justice of India ➢His term is fixed for 5 years ➢He is eligible for re election any number of times. ➢If the President is not able to discharge his functions, Vice-President will be acting as the President. ➢The President will address his resignation letter to the Vice-President ➢President is entitled for the salary, pension, accommodation and all other allowances as per the President’s Emoluments Act. ➢He is immune from all civil and criminal action.
BMS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY AND MANAGEMENT 20-12-2023 Tejaswini.B,J, Assistant Professor 8 Functions/ Powers of the President
BMS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY AND MANAGEMENT 20-12-2023 9 Judicial Powers Pardon Reprive Respite Remit Commute

BMS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY AND MANAGEMENT 20-12-2023 Tejaswini.B,J, Assistant Professor 10 Impeachment of the President, Article 61 ➢Grounds for removal- Violation of the constitution ➢Procedure- a resolution for removal should be supported by 1/4th of the total members of the house where it is initiated(either house of the Parliament), advance notice of 14 days should be given to the president and if the resolution is passed by 2/3rd members of both the houses, then he is removed from his post. ➢No President is impeached in India till today.

BMS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY AND MANAGEMENT 20-12-2023 11 Vice- President • Qualifications • Elections • Oath,terms and conditions of service • Powers/Functions • Removal Qualifications ➢He/She should be a citizen of India ➢Should be more than 35 years of age ➢Should not hold any office of profit ➢Should be qualified to be a member of Rajya Sabha Elections Vice President is indirectly elected by an electoral college which comprises of elected members of both the houses of the Parliament.

BMS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY AND MANAGEMENT 20-12-2023 12 Oath, terms and conditions, privileges of the Vice President ➢Vice President is administered oath by President of India ➢His term is fixed for 5 years ➢The Vice President will address his resignation letter to the President ➢He is entitled for the salary, pension, accommodation and all other allowances as per the Vice President’s Emoluments Act. Powers/Functions ➢Takes over the office of President in cases of death/resignation/removal or impeachment of the President/ President is unable to discharge his duties owing to absence, illness or any other cause. ➢Ex- Officio Chairman of Rajya Sabha Removal Vice President can be removed from office by a resolution of the Rajya Sabha, passed by a majority of its members at that time and agreed to by Lok sabha members.

BMS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY AND MANAGEMENT 20-12-2023 Tejaswini.B,J, Assistant Professor 13 Prime Minister and the Council of Ministers ➢Prime Minister and CoM – real executives ➢PM- qualification, powers and functions ➢CoM- individual responsibility, collective responsibility ➢size of the CoM shall not exceed 15% of the strength of Lok Sabha as per the 91st Constitutional amendment act 2003

BMS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY AND MANAGEMENT 20-12-2023 Tejaswini.B,J, Assistant Professor 16 Appointment: Every Judge of a Supreme Court is appointed by the President in consultation with the Judges of the Supreme Court, High Courts and the Council of Ministers. Tenure: A supreme court Judge will serve until he retires which is at the age of 65 Years Jurisdiction : ➢Original ➢Appellate ➢Special leave appeal ➢Writ jurisdiction ➢Advisory jurisdiction ➢Judicial review ➢Judicial Activism Impeachment : A judge of the Supreme Court can be removed on the grounds of proved mis-behavior and incapacity by passing an impeachment resolution in the Parliament with 2/3rd majority and approved by the President.

BMS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY AND MANAGEMENT 20-12-2023 Tejaswini.B,J, Assistant Professor 20 State Judiciary- High Courts Parliament can establish a common High Court for two or more states or Union Territory. At present there are 25 High courts in India • Composition • Qualifications • Appointment • Tenure • Jurisdiction • Impeachment Composition- High Court consists of a Chief Justice and other judges whose number may be fixed from time to time by the Parliament Qualifications – ➢Citizen of India ➢Served as a Judge in any of the lower courts for not less than 10 years ➢Practiced as an Advocate for not less than 10 years

BMS INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY AND MANAGEMENT 20-12-2023 Tejaswini.B,J, Assistant Professor 21 Appointment- Appointed by the President of India in consultation with Chief Justice of India, Governor of the respective state and Chief Justice of the respective state Tenure - A High court Judge serves until he retires which is at the age of 62 Years Jurisdiction : ➢Original ➢Appellate ➢Writ jurisdiction ➢Court of record ➢Court of superintendence ➢Judicial review ➢Judicial Activism Impeachment : A judge of the High Court can be removed on the grounds of proved mis-behavior and incapacity by passing an impeachment resolution in the Parliament with 2/3rd majority and approved by the President.

while Loop – Examples 1.Print 1 to 5 i = 1 while i <= 5: print(i) i += 1 Output: 1 2 3 4 5 2. Countdown from 3 n = 3 while n > 0: print("Countdown:", n) n -= 1 Output: Countdown: 3 Countdown: 2 Countdown: 1 3. Sum of digits of 123 num = 123 sum_digits = 0 while num > 0: sum_digits += num % 10 num //= 10 print("Sum:", sum_digits) Output: Sum: 6
4. Smallest divisor of 12 num = 12 i = 2 while i <= num: if num % i == 0: print("Smallest divisor:", i) break i += 1 Output: Smallest divisor: 2

elif Statement – 5 Examples 1. Grade system score = 85 if score >= 90: print("A") elif score >= 80: print("B") elif score >= 70: print("C") else: print("Fail") Output: B 2. Temperature check temp = 30 if temp > 35: print("Too hot!") elif temp > 25: print("Warm") else: print("Cool") Output: Warm 3. Number type x = 0 if x > 0: print("Positive") elif x < 0: print("Negative") else: print("Zero") Output: Zero

4. Traffic signal light = "yellow" if light == "red": print("Stop") elif light == "yellow": print("Wait") elif light == "green": print("Go") Output: Wait 5. Age category age = 15 if age < 13: print("Child") elif age < 18: print("Teen") else: print("Adult") Output: Teen

for Loop – Examples with Output 1. Print 1 to 5 for i in range(1, 6): print(i) Output: 1 2 3 4 5 2. Loop through list colors = ["red", "green", "blue"] for color in colors: print(color) Output: red green blue

3. Sum of 1 to 5 total = 0 for i in range(1, 6): total += i print("Total:", total) Output: Total: 15 4. Print even numbers for i in range(2, 11, 2): print(i) Output: 2 4 6 8 10 5. Reverse string text = "hello" for char in reversed(text): print(char) Output: o l l e h

break Statement – Examples 1. Stop at 5 for i in range(10): if i == 5: break print(i) Output: 0 1 2 3 4 2. Prime check for 7 n = 7 for i in range(2, n): if n % i == 0: print("Not prime") break else: print("Prime") Output: Prime

3. Find first even nums = [3, 7, 4, 5] for n in nums: if n % 2 == 0: print("First even:", n) break Output: First even: 4 4. Stop on 0 input while True: n = int(input("Enter number: ")) if n == 0: break print(n * 2) Sample Input: 3 4 0 Output: 6 8

continue Statement – Examples 1. Skip odd numbers for i in range(1, 6): if i % 2 != 0: continue print(i) Output: 2 4 2. Skip “skip” words = ["one", "skip", "two"] for word in words: if word == "skip": continue print(word) Output: one two

3. Skip zero nums = [10, 0, 5] for n in nums: if n == 0: continue print("Result:", 100 / n) Output: Result: 10.0 Result: 20.0 4. Skip empty input inputs = ["hi", "", "hello"] for text in inputs: if text == "": continue print("You typed:", text) Output: You typed: hi You typed: hello 5. Skip vowels text = "hello" for ch in text: if ch in "aeiou": continue print(ch) Output: h l l

4. Read a multi-digit number (as chars) from the console. Develop a program to print a the frequency of each digit with a suitable message. # Read a multi-digit number as a string number = input("Enter a multi-digit number: ") # Print the frequency of each digit for digit in '@123456789': count = number.count(digit) if count > ®@: print("Digit", digit, "occurs", count, "times") [4] Enter a multi-digit number: 2345563424454569611786 Digit @ occurs 1 times Digit 1 occurs 2 times Digit 2 occurs 2 times Digit 3 occurs 2 times Digit 4 occurs 5 times Digit 5 occurs 4 times Digit 6 occurs 3 times Digit 7 occurs 1 times Digit 8 occurs 1 times Digit § occurs 1 times

number = input("Enter a multi-digit number:") e This line prompts the user to input a number (with possibly more than one digit). e The input is stored as a string in the variable number . for digit in '@123456789"': e This line starts a for loop that iterates over each character in the string '@123456789' , which represents all possible digits from 0 to 9. *« So, digit willtake the values '@', '1', '2',.., '9" one by one. count = number.count(digit) e For each digit, this line counts how many times that digit appears in the user-input string number . * The .count() method returns the number of occurrences of the specified value. if count > 6: * This line checks whether the current digit appeared at least once in the number.

print("Digit", digit, "occurs", count, "times") If the digit is found (i-e., count > @), this line prints how many times it occurs. Example: If the user inputs: 11235813 The output will be: bash Digit 1 occurs 3 times Digit 2 occurs 1 times Digit 3 occurs 2? times Digit 5 occurs 1 times Digit 8 occurs 1 times

1. Create a Python List # a list of three elements ages = [19, 26, 29] print(ages) 2. List Items of Different Types # a list containing strings, numbers and another list student = ['Jack', 32, 'Computer Science', [2, 4]] print(student) # an empty list empty_list = [] print(empty_list) LISTS

4. Access List Elements languages = ['Python', 'Swift', 'C++'] # access the first element print('languages[0] =', languages[0]) # access the third element print('languages[2] =', languages[2]) 5. Negative Indexing languages = ['Python', 'Swift', 'C++'] # access the last item print('languages[-1] =', languages[-1]) # access the third last item print('languages[-3] =', languages[-3])

6. Slicing of a List in Python my_list = ['p', 'r', 'o', 'g', 'r', 'a', 'm'] print("my_list =", my_list) # get a list with items from index 2 to index 4 (index 5 is not included) print("my_list[2: 5] =", my_list[2: 5]) # get a list with items from index 2 to index -3 (index -2 is not included) print("my_list[2: -2] =", my_list[2: -2]) # get a list with items from index 0 to index 2 (index 3 is not included) print("my_list[0: 3] =", my_list[0: 3]) my_list = ['p', 'r', 'o', 'g', 'r', 'a', 'm'] print("my_list =", my_list) # get a list with items from index 5 to last print("my_list[5: ] =", my_list[5: ]) # get a list from the first item to index -5 print("my_list[: -4] =", my_list[: -4]) # get a list from start to end items print("my_list[:] =", my_list[:])

Add Elements to a Python List fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'orange'] print('Original List:', fruits) fruits.append('cherry') print('Updated List:', fruits) Add Elements at the Specified Index fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'orange'] print("Original List:", fruits) fruits.insert(2, 'cherry') print("Updated List:", fruits)

Change List Items colors = ['Red', 'Black', 'Green'] print('Original List:', colors) # change the first item to 'Purple' colors[2] = 'Purple' # change the third item to 'Blue' colors[2] = 'Blue' print('Updated List:', colors) Remove an Item From a List numbers = [2,4,7,9] # remove 4 from the list numbers.remove(4) print(numbers)

names = ['John', 'Eva', 'Laura', 'Nick', 'Jack'] # delete the item at index 1 del names[1] print(names) # delete items from index 1 to index 2 del names[1: 3] print(names) # delete the entire list del names # Error! List doesn't exist. print(names) Remove One or More Elements of a List

import random messages = ['It is certain', 'It is decidedly so', 'Yes definitely', 'Reply hazy try again', 'Ask again later', 'Concentrate and ask again', 'My reply is no', 'Outlook not so good', 'Very doubtful'] print(messages[random.randint(0, len(messages) - 1)]) EXAMPLE PROGRAM: MAGIC 8 BALL WITH A LIST

Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS Email: vishwakiran@bmsit.in 1. The Integer Data Type What it is? An integer is a whole number (no fractional part) and can be positive, negative, or zero. Examples: -10, 0, 25, 100000 When and Where it can be applied? Use integers when dealing with: Countable quantities (e.g., number of customers, vehicle count, attempts) Values that do not need decimal precision Applications: 📌 Banking: Account numbers Number of failed login attempts Number of transactions Automotive: Engine RPM Number of wheels Gear position How to apply (syntax)? # Declaration x = 10 y = -200 z = 0 # Operations sum = x + 5 Examples Banking: 🔒 failed_attempts = 3 print("Access blocked after", failed_attempts, "failed attempts.") Automotive: 🚗 gear_position = 5 print("Current gear:", gear_position) Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M

2. 💠 The Floating-Point Data Type What it is? A floating-point (float) number is a number with a decimal point or written in exponential form. Examples: 3.14, -0.99, 100.0, 1.2e3 When and Where it can be applied? Use floats when: Decimal precision is required Measuring or computing with accuracy Applications: 📌 Banking: Interest rate (e.g., 6.5%) Account balance (e.g., 1325.75 ₹ ) Automotive: Fuel efficiency (km/l) Temperature readings (87.3 °C) Speed (65.5 km/h) How to apply (syntax)? # Declaration balance = 12345.67 temperature = -5.3 # Operations new_balance = balance + 100.25 Examples Banking: 🔒 balance = 4321.50 interest_rate = 5.5 # percentage interest = (balance * interest_rate) / 100 print("Interest:", interest) Automotive: 🚗 engine_temp = 92.7 speed = 64.3 print("Speed:", speed, "km/h") Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M

Summary Table 📊 Feature Integer (int) Floating-point (float) Values Whole numbers Decimal numbers Examples 10, -3, 0 3.14, -0.01, 1.2e3 Use Case (Banking) Account No, Attempt Count Balance, Interest rate Use Case (Automotive) Gear position, RPM Speed, Fuel %, Engine Temp Operations +, -, *, //, %, ** +, -, *, /, ** 1. String Data Type What it is? A string is a sequence of characters enclosed in single quotes (' ') or double quotes (" "). It can include letters, digits, spaces, and symbols. When and where it can be applied? Use strings to represent textual data like: Names, addresses, messages, identifiers, codes, logs, timestamps Applications: 📌 Banking: Customer names, account types, transaction remarks Automotive: Vehicle status logs, error messages, model names How to apply (syntax)? # Declaration name = "Ravi" vehicle_model = 'Maruti Swift' Examples Banking: 🔒 account_type = "Savings" customer_name = "Anjali" print("Account Type:", account_type) Automotive: 🚗 model = "Hyundai Verna" status = "Engine Check OK" print("Vehicle Model:", model) Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M
Verna" status = "Engine Check OK" print("Vehicle Model:", model) Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M

2. String Concatenation What it is? Concatenation means joining two or more strings using the + operator. When and where it can be applied? Used to: Build messages Combine identifiers (e.g., user IDs or logs) Format output dynamically Applications: 📌 Banking: Combine customer name with messages Automotive: Combine sensor name with alert message How to apply (syntax)? full_name = first_name + " " + last_name message = "Speed is " + str(speed) + " km/h" Examples Banking: 🔒 first_name = "Rahul" last_name = "Kumar" welcome_msg = "Hello, " + first_name + " " + last_name print(welcome_msg) Automotive: 🚗 sensor = "Temperature" value = "85°C" alert = sensor + " reading is " + value print(alert) 3. String Replication What it is? Replication means repeating a string multiple times using the * operator. When and where it can be applied? Used for: Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M

Formatting output (lines, headers, separators) Simulation messages or placeholders Applications: 📌 Banking: Print lines in receipts or reports Automotive: Repeat status or test messages in logs How to apply (syntax)? separator = "-" * 30 print(separator) Examples Banking: 🔒 print("Transaction Receipt") print("=" * 40) Automotive: 🚗 print("Diagnostic Log") print("*" * 50) Summary Table Concept Operator Syntax Example Domain Example String Declaration – name = "Anil" "account_type = 'Savings'" String Concatenation + "Hello " + name "Engine Temp: " + temp String Replication * "=" * 20 "*" * 50 # log separator" 1. Storing Values in Variables What it is? Variables are named containers used to store values (data) like numbers, text, etc., in Python. You can assign any type of value to a variable. When and Where it can be Applied? Use variables when: You need to store intermediate results You want to reuse data or track state of a system Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M
CSBS, BMSIT&M

Applications: 📌 Banking: Store customer balance, interest rate, account status Automotive: Store engine temperature, speed, gear position How to Apply (Syntax)? balance = 5000 # Integer account_type = "Savings" # String engine_temp = 95.6 # Float Examples Banking: 🔒 customer_name = "Arjun" account_balance = 12000 Automotive: 🚗 speed = 65.4 gear = 3 status = "Running" 2. Your First Program What it is? A simple Python script that demonstrates variable assignment and output using print() function. When and Where it can be Applied? Ideal for beginners Foundation for debugging, testing Forms basis of larger programs Applications: 📌 Banking: Print account summary Automotive: Display vehicle diagnostic summary How to Apply (Syntax)? # First Program name = "Ravi" print("Welcome", name) Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M

Examples Banking: 🔒 account_holder = "Meera" balance = 8750 print("Account Holder:", account_holder) print("Available Balance:", balance) Automotive: 🚗 vehicle = "Tata Nexon" speed = 75.3 print("Vehicle:", vehicle) print("Speed:", speed, "km/h") 3. Dissecting Your Program What it is? This means breaking down each part of your Python program to understand what it does line-by- line. When and Where it can be Applied? Use this technique to learn and debug code. Essential in training, education, and testing scenarios. Applications: 📌 Banking: Understanding transaction logic Automotive: Diagnosing logic for error detection or status update How to Apply? Take this example: # Program to calculate total balance name = "Amit" # Stores customer's name balance = 10000 # Initial balance deposit = 2500 # Amount to deposit balance = balance + deposit # New balance after deposit print("Hello", name) # Greeting message print("Updated Balance:", balance) # Show updated balance Examples with Dissection Banking: 🔒 # Program to show balance update name = "Amit" # Variable storing name balance = 10000 # Original balance deposit = 2500 # New deposit balance += deposit # Update balance print("Hello",

ion Banking: 🔒 # Program to show balance update name = "Amit" # Variable storing name balance = 10000 # Original balance deposit = 2500 # New deposit balance += deposit # Update balance print("Hello", name) print("Updated Balance:", balance) Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M

Automotive: 🚗 # Program to calculate safe speed range car_model = "Hyundai i20" speed = 70 # Current speed max_safe_speed = 80 print("Car:", car_model) print("Speed:", speed, "km/h") if speed < max_safe_speed: print("Status: Safe Driving") else: print("Warning: Over Speed!") Summary Table Concept What it does Domain Use Example Storing Values Assign data to variables Customer name, Engine speed name = "Asha" First Program Run simple code using print and vars Greet user, Show status print("Hello", name) Dissecting Program Explain each line of code Debug banking/vehicle logic balance += deposit 1. Boolean Values What it is? Boolean values represent truth values: True False They are used to control the flow of a program through decisions (conditions). When and Where it Can Be Applied? When performing logical checks, comparisons, or deciding actions. Applications: 📌 Banking: Check if balance is sufficient Validate login attempt Automotive: Detect if engine temperature is too high Check if headlights should be ON Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M
Check if headlights should be ON Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M

How to Apply (Syntax)? status = True if status: print("Proceed") Examples Banking: 🔒 is_verified = True if is_verified: print("Transaction Approved") Automotive: 🚗 engine_on = False if not engine_on: print("Engine is off") 2. Comparison Operators What it is? Used to compare two values and return a Boolean result (True or False). Operator Meaning == Equal to != Not equal to > Greater than < Less than >= Greater than or equal to <= Less than or equal to When and Where it Can Be Applied? Used in: if-else, while, for loops Any decision-making part of a program Applications: 📌 Banking: Check if withdrawal amount is within balance Automotive: Check if speed is above safety limit How to Apply (Syntax)? balance = 5000 if balance > 1000: Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M

print("Eligible for loan") Examples Banking: 🔒 balance = 3000 withdraw = 2500 if balance >= withdraw: print("Withdrawal permitted") else: print("Insufficient funds") Automotive: 🚗 speed = 90 if speed > 80: print("Warning: Over-speeding") Combined Use Syntax: if condition: # condition returns Boolean do_something() Example – Banking: pin_entered = 1234 correct_pin = 1234 if pin_entered == correct_pin: print("Access Granted") else: print("Invalid PIN") Example – Automotive: engine_temp = 105 if engine_temp > 100: print("Engine Overheating Warning!") 1. Boolean Operators What it is? Boolean operators are used to combine or manipulate boolean values. They return either True or False. Operator Description Example and True if both conditions are true A and B or True if at least one is true A or B not Inverts the boolean value not A When and Where it Can Be Applied? To create complex conditions in decision-making. When you need multiple checks in one statement. Applications: 📌 Banking: Check if balance is sufficient and account is active. Check if user is admin or manager. Automotive: Check if speed > 100 and temperature > 90. Alert if door is open or seatbelt is not worn. How to Apply (Syntax)? if condition1 and condition2: # do something Examples Banking: 🔒 balance = 15000 account_status = "Active" if balance > 10000 and account_status == "Active": print("Eligible for premium account") Automotive: 🚗 speed = 105 engine_temp = 98 if speed > 100 and engine_temp > 95: print("Alert: High Speed and Engine Heat!") Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M
Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M

2. Mixing Boolean and Comparison Operators What it is? You can combine comparisons using boolean operators to form compound conditions. Example: if (balance >= 5000 and account_type == "Savings"): When and Where it Can Be Applied? Useful in nested validations Helps shorten logic with readable conditions Applications: 📌 Banking: Apply interest only if balance ≥ 5000 ₹ and account is “Savings” Automotive: Alert driver if (seatbelt not fastened) or (door not closed) How to Apply (Syntax)? if (comparison1) and (comparison2): action() Examples Banking: 🔒 balance = 6000 account_type = "Savings" kyc_verified = True if balance > 5000 and account_type == "Savings" and kyc_verified: print("Eligible for Interest Credit") Automotive: 🚗 door_closed = False seatbelt_fastened = True if not door_closed or not seatbelt_fastened: print("Warning: Safety Protocol Violation") Summary Table Expression Result Notes True and True True Both must be true True and False False One false makes entire False False or True True At least one true makes it True Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M
alse or True True At least one true makes it True Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M

Expression Result Notes not False True Negation of False balance > 5000 and kyc_verified Varies Used in banking speed > 90 and engine_temp > 95 Varies Used in automotive Tip: Use Parentheses for Clarity if (speed > 90 and engine_temp > 100) or driver_drowsy: print("Activate Auto-Brake System") 1. Condition What it is? A condition is an expression that evaluates to either True or False. It determines whether a block of code should be executed or skipped. When and Where it Can Be Applied? Anytime decision-making is needed in a program. Applications: 📌 Banking: If balance > withdrawal amount → allow withdrawal Automotive: If speed > speed_limit → issue warning How to Apply (Syntax)? if condition: # block of code Examples Banking: 🔒 balance = 12000 withdraw = 5000 if balance >= withdraw: print("Withdrawal Approved") Automotive: 🚗 speed = 90 if speed > 80: print("Warning: Over Speeding!") Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M

2. Block of Code What it is? A block of code is a group of statements that run together under a condition, defined by indentation (usually 4 spaces in Python). When and Where it Can Be Applied? Blocks are used under if, elif, else, for, while etc. Only executes if the associated condition is True. How to Apply (Syntax)? if condition: # This is a block (4-space indent) do_something() do_something_else() Note: In Python, indentation is not optional – it defines the block. Examples Banking: 🔒 balance = 8000 min_balance = 5000 if balance >= min_balance: print("Eligible for Loan") print("Proceed to next step") Automotive: 🚗 engine_temp = 102 if engine_temp > 100: print("Overheating Detected") print("Activating Cooling System") Summary Table Element Role in Flow Control Example (Banking) Example (Automotive) Condition Decides whether code block runs if balance > 5000: if speed > 80: Block Executes only if condition is True print("Loan Approved") print("Turn on Fan") Indentation Defines code block scope 4 spaces or a tab under if, else, etc. Required under all control structures Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M
e block scope 4 spaces or a tab under if, else, etc. Required under all control structures Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M

1. Program Execution What it is? Program execution in Python refers to the sequential line-by-line interpretation and execution of code by the Python interpreter. When and Where It Can Be Applied? Applied always when running a Python script or module. The interpreter starts at the first line and proceeds downward, unless altered by flow control, function calls, loops, or exceptions. Applications: 📌 Banking: Transaction processing based on steps Automotive: Sequential checking of sensors and actuators How to Use: Python programs can be executed: bash myscript.py Example: print("Program Started") balance = 5000 print("Balance:", balance) 2. Flow Control Statements Flow control changes the natural top-to-bottom execution of a program using conditions. 🧩a) if Statement What it is? The if statement checks a single condition, and executes the block if it evaluates to True. When and Where to Use? Use when there is one condition to verify. Applications: 📌 Banking: Check if user is verified Automotive: Check if engine temperature is above threshold Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M
d Automotive: Check if engine temperature is above threshold Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M

Syntax: if condition: # block of code Examples: Banking: 🔒 balance = 10000 if balance > 0: print("Proceed with transaction") Automotive: 🚗 speed = 90 if speed > 80: print("Over-speeding detected!") 🧩b) if-else Statement What it is? Handles two branches — one block runs if condition is True, the else block runs if it's False. When and Where to Use? When you need to take action in both outcomes. Applications: 📌 Banking: Withdraw success or show insufficient funds Automotive: Alert or confirm normal engine condition Syntax: if condition: # code if true else: # code if false Examples: Banking: 🔒 balance = 3000 withdraw = 4000 if balance >= withdraw: print("Transaction Approved") else: print("Insufficient Funds") Automotive: 🚗 engine_temp = 85 if engine_temp > 100: Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M

print("Overheating Warning!") else: print("Engine Temperature Normal") c) if-elif-else Ladder What it is? Used when there are multiple conditions, checked one-by-one. When and Where to Use? When you need multi-level decision making. Applications: 📌 Banking: Interest rate based on balance ranges Automotive: Gear suggestion based on speed Syntax: if condition1: # block 1 elif condition2: # block 2 else: # block N Examples: Banking: 🔒 balance = 120000 if balance < 5000: print("No Interest") elif balance < 100000: print("Interest Rate: 4%") else: print("Interest Rate: 6%") Automotive: 🚗 speed = 45 if speed < 20: print("Gear 1") elif speed < 40: print("Gear 2") elif speed < 60: print("Gear 3") else: print("Gear 4 or 5") Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M

Summary Table Statement Description Banking Example Automotive Example if Executes when condition is True if balance > 0: if speed > 80: if-else Two outcomes (True/False) Approve or reject withdrawal Show warning or OK status if-elif-else Multiple decision branches Interest based on balance Gear suggestion based on speed 1. while Loop What it is? The while loop repeatedly executes a block of code as long as a condition is True. When and Where to Use? When you don’t know the exact number of iterations in advance. Best suited for sensor monitoring, login attempts, or repeated actions until a condition changes. Applications: 📌 Banking: Allow up to 3 login attempts Automotive: Monitor sensor value until a threshold is reached Syntax: while condition: # block of code Examples Banking: 🔒 attempts = 0 while attempts < 3: pin = input("Enter PIN: ") if pin == "1234": print("Access Granted") break else: print("Wrong PIN") attempts += 1 Automotive: 🚗 engine_temp = 90 while engine_temp < 100: print("Engine Temperature:", engine_temp) engine_temp += 2 Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M
print("Engine Temperature:", engine_temp) engine_temp += 2 Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M

2. break Statement What it is? Used to immediately exit a loop (either for or while) when a condition is met. When and Where to Use? To stop execution prematurely when some condition is met. Applications: 📌 Banking: Stop login attempts after correct PIN Automotive: Stop checking sensor when critical value is hit Syntax: while condition: if some_condition: break Examples Banking: 🔒 while True: pin = input("Enter PIN: ") if pin == "4321": print("Access granted") break print("Try again") Automotive: 🚗 speed = 0 while True: speed += 10 if speed > 100: print("Critical Speed Reached:", speed) break 3. continue Statement What it is? Skips the current iteration and jumps to the next cycle of the loop. When and Where to Use? When you want to skip specific conditions and keep looping. Applications: 📌 Banking: Skip transactions below 1000 while summarizing ₹ Automotive: Ignore invalid sensor values like -1 Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M

Syntax: while condition: if skip_condition: continue # code that runs if not skipped Examples Banking: 🔒 transactions = [500, 1200, 8000, 300] i = 0 while i < len(transactions): if transactions[i] < 1000: i += 1 continue print("Processed:", transactions[i]) i += 1 Automotive: 🚗 sensor_data = [70, -1, 90, 85] i = 0 while i < len(sensor_data): if sensor_data[i] == -1: i += 1 continue print("Valid Sensor Value:", sensor_data[i]) i += 1 Summary Table Concept Description Use in Banking Use in Automotive while Loops while condition is True Allow login attempts Monitor sensor until safe value break Exits the loop early Exit on correct PIN entry Stop loop at critical speed continue Skips current loop iteration Ignore small transactions Skip invalid sensor values 1. for Loops What it is? A for loop is used to iterate over a sequence (like a list, tuple, string, or range) and execute a block of code once for each item in that sequence. When and Where to Use? When the number of iterations is known or countable Efficient for iterating through items in a list or performing an action a fixed number of times Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M
wn or countable Efficient for iterating through items in a list or performing an action a fixed number of times Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M

Applications: 📌 Banking: Loop over transactions or customer records Automotive: Loop through sensor values, log entries Syntax: for variable in sequence: # block of code Examples Banking: 🔒 transactions = [2000, -1500, 3000, -500] for txn in transactions: print("Transaction Amount:", txn) Automotive: 🚗 speeds = [40, 50, 65, 80, 90] for speed in speeds: print("Speed logged:", speed, "km/h") 2. range() Function What it is? The range() function returns a sequence of numbers, starting from a start (default 0) to a stop (exclusive), with a defined step (default 1). When and Where to Use? When you want to loop a fixed number of times Best used with for loops for indexed iteration Applications: 📌 Banking: Generate monthly reports for N months Automotive: Log data every N seconds or simulate sensor readings Syntax: range(stop) range(start, stop) range(start, stop, step) Examples Banking: 🔒 # Print statement for 6 months for month in range(1, 7): print("Month", month, ": Interest credited") Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M
") Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M

Automotive: 🚗 # Simulate RPM from 1000 to 5000 in steps of 1000 for rpm in range(1000, 6000, 1000): print("Engine RPM:", rpm) Summary Table Concept Description Banking Use Case Automotive Use Case for loop Iterates over items in a sequence Iterate through account transactions Iterate through speed or RPM values range() Generates a sequence of numbers Loop over months, customer IDs Simulate time steps, sensor values range(5) 0 to 4 5 transactions 5 sensor readings range(1, 4) 1 to 3 Generate 3-month report RPM levels: 1000, 2000, 3000 range(0, 10, 2) Even numbers between 0 and 9 Report every 2 days Speed simulation in steps Combined Example: Banking: 🔒 # Simulate a mini statement amounts = [5000, -2000, -500, 7000] for i in range(len(amounts)): print(f"Transaction {i+1}: {amounts[i]}") ₹ Automotive: 🚗 # Simulate log of temperature readings for i in range(1, 6): print("Sensor Reading", i, ": Temperature OK") 1. What is range() with start, stop, step? The range() function in Python is used to generate a sequence of numbers. It can accept up to 3 arguments: 🔹range(start, stop, step) start – starting value (inclusive) stop – ending value (exclusive) step – difference between each num

d to generate a sequence of numbers. It can accept up to 3 arguments: 🔹range(start, stop, step) start – starting value (inclusive) stop – ending value (exclusive) step – difference between each number (default is +1) If only 1 value is passed, it’s treated as stop, and start defaults to 0. Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M

2. When and Where It Can Be Applied Used in controlled loops, such as: Fixed repetition Skipping values in a pattern Creating number sequences for reports, logs, or time intervals Applications: Banking: Generate transaction IDs Create monthly interest schedules Automotive: Simulate RPM, speed increases Periodic data logging (e.g., every 5 seconds) 3. How to Apply: Syntax & Explanation 3.1. 🔸 range(stop) for i in range(5): # Equivalent to range(0, 5) print(i) Output: 0 1 2 3 4 3.2. 🔸 range(start, stop) for i in range(2, 6): print(i) Output: 2 3 4 5 3.3. 🔸 range(start, stop, step) for i in range(10, 0, -2): print(i) Output: 10 8 6 4 2 4. Domain Examples 🔒Banking Example 1: Generate 6-month report for month in range(1, 7): print("Month", month, ": Interest credited") Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M

🔒Banking Example 2: Transaction ID skipping for txn_id in range(1001, 1011, 2): # ID steps of 2 print("Transaction ID:", txn_id) 🚗Automotive Example 1: Simulate increasing speed for speed in range(0, 101, 20): # From 0 to 100 in steps of 20 print("Speed:", speed, "km/h") 🚗Automotive Example 2: RPM deceleration for rpm in range(6000, 1000, -1000): print("RPM:", rpm) Summary Table Syntax Description Example Output range(5) 0 to 4 0 1 2 3 4 range(2, 6) 2 to 5 2 3 4 5 range(1, 10, 2) Odd numbers from 1 to 9 1 3 5 7 9 range(10, 0, -2) Countdown from 10 to 2 10 8 6 4 2 range(start, stop, 1) Default stepping Increments by 1 1. Importing Modules What it is? A module is a file containing Python code (functions, classes, or variables). Importing a module allows you to reuse code from built-in libraries or custom scripts. When and Where to Use? When you want to organize code, reuse functionality, or access standard libraries (e.g., math, datetime, os, random) Helps with modularity, reusability, and clarity Applications: 📌 Banking: Use datetime to calculate interest periods Use json to store user profiles securely Automotive: Use time to simulate sensor readings at intervals Notes Prepa

and clarity Applications: 📌 Banking: Use datetime to calculate interest periods Use json to store user profiles securely Automotive: Use time to simulate sensor readings at intervals Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M

Use math for calculations (e.g., fuel efficiency) How to Apply (Syntax) import module_name You can then access its functions using: module_name.function_name() Examples Banking – Using 🔒 datetime: import datetime today = datetime.date.today() print("Date of Transaction:", today) Automotive – Using 🚗 time: import time for i in range(3): print("Sensor Reading", i) time.sleep(2) # waits for 2 seconds 2. from ... import ... Statement What it is? A shortcut to import only specific parts (functions, classes, variables) from a module. It avoids the need to use the module prefix. When and Where to Use? When only a specific function or class is needed Makes code cleaner and more readable Applications: 📌 Banking: Import only date from datetime for transactions Automotive: Import only sleep from time module to simulate delays Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M

How to Apply (Syntax) from module_name import function_name You can now use: function_name() # No module prefix needed Examples Banking – Use 🔒 from datetime import date: from datetime import date print("Today's Date:", date.today()) Automotive – Use 🚗 from time import sleep: from time import sleep for i in range(3): print("Monitoring engine...") sleep(1) Summary Table Syntax Description Example import module Imports the whole module import datetime module.function() Access function using module name datetime.date.today() from module import function Imports only the required function from time import sleep function() Use directly without prefix sleep(2) 1. What is sys.exit()? What it is? sys.exit() is a function provided by Python’s sys module. It is used to immediately terminate the execution of a Python program before it naturally ends. When and Where to Use? When a critical error or invalid condition occurs When you want to forcefully stop execution under specific scenarios Applications: 📌 Banking: Exit program if login fails after 3 attempts Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M
ations: 📌 Banking: Exit program if login fails after 3 attempts Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M

Stop transaction processing if KYC is not verified Automotive: Stop diagnostics if essential sensor data is missing Exit loop if hardware not responding 2. How to Apply (Syntax) Step-by-step: import sys if condition_to_exit: sys.exit("Reason for exiting") The message in 💡 sys.exit("...") is optional and is printed to stderr. 3. Banking Domain Example import sys pin_attempts = 0 while pin_attempts < 3: pin = input("Enter your PIN: ") if pin == "1234": print("Access granted") break else: print("Incorrect PIN") pin_attempts += 1 if pin_attempts == 3: sys.exit("Too many failed attempts. Program terminated.") 4. Automotive Domain Example import sys sensor_connected = False if not sensor_connected: sys.exit("Error: Critical sensor not connected. Aborting diagnostics.") else: print("Running diagnostics...") Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M

Summary Table Concept Description Example Output sys.exit() Terminates the program immediately Too many failed attempts. Program terminated. Use case On error, failed login, missing hardware Security breach or hardware fault Requires import sys Must be called before use Tip: 🔐 Use sys.exit() inside: Loops if statements Error-handling blocks (try...except) Objective: Let the user guess a number between 1 and 10. The program: Randomly selects a number Prompts user repeatedly until correct Shows a success message or exits after limited attempts 1. What It Is A beginner-friendly, interactive program that demonstrates core Python logic using conditions, loops, and modules. 2. When and Where to Use This kind of game: Is great for learning flow control Can be adapted for secure input, attempt limits, or random simulations Real-world use: Banking: Security PIN guessing simulation Automotive: Password entry for diagnostics Notes Prepared by: Dr. Vishwa Kiran S, Dept of CSBS, BMSIT&M

How to Apply – Code Example import random secret_number = random.randint(1, 10) attempts = 0 max_attempts = 5 print("Welcome to Guess the Number Game!") print("I'm thinking of a number between 1 and 10.") while attempts < max_attempts: guess = int(input("Take a guess: ")) attempts += 1 if guess < secret_number: print("Too low!") elif guess > secret_number: print("Too high!") else: print(" Good job!
